blocks.cap: Here is the whole move in one picture. Find the number that turns the denominator into a power of ten, multiply the top and bottom by it, then read the digits straight off into their places. The bottom is \(20\), and \(20 = 2^2 \times 5\), so it is only missing one more \(5\) to become \(2^2 \times 5^2 = 100\). Multiplying top and bottom by \(\tfrac{5}{5}\) keeps the value the same, and it sends \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) to \(\tfrac{55}{100}\). Once the denominator is \(100\), the \(5\) and \(5\) just drop into the tenths and hundredths columns to give \(0.55\).

blocks.x: Write \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) as a decimal. The denominator \(20\) does not reach \(10\), so aim for the next power of ten it divides into evenly.
hints: You cannot reach 10, so aim higher. What is the next power of ten after 10, and does 20 fit into it a whole number of times?
Since \(20 \times 5 = 100\), multiply both the top and the bottom by 5. That gives \(\tfrac{55}{100}\), and a denominator of 100 reads straight off as hundredths.
blocks.sol: The goal is to give the denominator a value of \(10\), \(100\), \(1{,}000\), or some other power of ten, because those are exactly the denominators that read off cleanly as decimals. Twenty is not one of them, so the question becomes which power of ten it divides into evenly. It will not fit into \(10\), since \(10\) is smaller than \(20\). The next one up is \(100\), and \(100 \div 20 = 5\) with nothing left over. That is how we knew to reach for \(5\). The bottom has to grow from \(20\) to \(100\), and \(5\) is the number that carries it there. Whatever we do to the bottom we must also do to the top, so multiply both by \(5\). $$\frac{11}{20} = \frac{11 \times 5}{20 \times 5} = \frac{55}{100}$$ Now the denominator is \(100\), so the fraction is \(55\) hundredths, which is \(0.55\). A quick check makes sure that fits. We know \(\tfrac{10}{20} = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5\), and \(\tfrac{11}{20}\) is one twentieth more than that, so a value just past \(0.5\) is exactly what we should land on. A little over half the scoop is raisins. \(\boxed{0.55}\)

blocks.x: A wrench is \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) inch wide. Since \(8=2^3\), supply three matching \(5\)s to reach \(2^3 \times 5^3=1{,}000\), then read off the decimal. Write \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) as a decimal.
hints: Decimals are just fractions whose denominators are powers of ten. So aim the bottom at a power of ten. You already have three 2s in the \(8\). How many 5s would you need to add so the 2s and 5s match up and form \(10\times 10\times 10\)?

To turn \(8 = 2^3\) into \(1{,}000 = 2^3\times 5^3\) you are missing exactly three 5s, and \(5^3 = 125\). Multiply the top and bottom of \(\tfrac{7}{8}\) by \(125\), then read off the three decimal places.
blocks.sol: The trick to turning a fraction into a decimal is to get a power of ten on the bottom, because \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\) is just another way of writing \(0.875\). So the whole job is reshaping the denominator into a power of ten without changing the value of the fraction. First break the bottom into primes. Here \(8 = 2\times 2\times 2 = 2^3\), which is three 2s and zero 5s. Now recall how powers of ten are built. Since \(10 = 2\times 5\), every power of ten needs its 2s and 5s in equal counts. For example \(1{,}000 = 10^3 = 2^3\times 5^3\), three of each. Compare that to what you have. Your denominator already supplies all three 2s, so it is short exactly three 5s, and \(5^3 = 125\). Supply the missing 5s by multiplying top and bottom by \(125\), which is the same as multiplying by \(1\), so the value does not change. $$\frac{7}{8} = \frac{7\times 125}{8\times 125} = \frac{875}{1000}.$$ A denominator of \(1{,}000\) means three decimal places, so \(\tfrac{875}{1000}\) reads as \(0.875\). The big idea is that you only ever have to top up whichever prime the bottom is short of. An all-2s denominator like \(8\) cleanly becomes a decimal precisely because you can fill in the matching 5s. \(\boxed{0.875}\)

blocks.x: Fraction to decimal by scaling. Scale the fraction up until the bottom is a power of ten, then read the numerator into its matching places, like $\tfrac{3}{4}=\tfrac{75}{100}=0.75$. A power of ten is $2^n\times5^n$ — a stack of $2$s matched one for one with the same many $5$s — so scaling never invents anything, it only supplies the missing partners: $8=2^3$ needs three $5$s, while $40$ already owns one $5$ and needs two more. The multiplier you reach for is exactly whatever $2$s and $5$s finish the matched set. A denominator carrying any other prime, like $3$ or $7$, can never be completed.
Factor each denominator: \(\frac{7}{16}\), \(\frac{9}{40}\), \(\frac{5}{12}\), \(\frac{3}{25}\). Only \(2\)s and \(5\)s allow termination. Which fraction cannot terminate? Give its denominator.
hints: Factor each denominator into primes. \(16 = 2^4\), \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\), \(25 = 5^2\), and \(12 = 2^2 \times 3\). Now look for any prime that is not a 2 or a 5.

A power of ten is \(2^n \times 5^n\), so it never contains a factor of 3. Three of the denominators use only 2s and 5s, so you can supply whatever is missing and climb to \(10\), \(100\), or \(1{,}000\). The denominator that hides a 3 can never be scaled into a power of ten, because no multiplier of 2s and 5s will ever cancel that 3.
blocks.sol: Factor every denominator into primes and the answer almost reveals itself. \(16 = 2^4\), so it is pure 2s. \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\), made of 2s and a 5. \(25 = 5^2\), pure 5s. And \(12 = 2^2 \times 3\), which carries a 3. The question is whether each denominator can be scaled up to a power of ten, because a power of ten in the denominator is exactly what a terminating decimal looks like. Since \(10^n = 2^n \times 5^n\), a power of ten is built from 2s and 5s and nothing else. For \(\dfrac{7}{16}\), multiply top and bottom by \(5^4\) to fill in the missing 5s and you reach \(10^4\), so it terminates. For \(\dfrac{9}{40}\), one extra factor of \(5^2\) balances the 2s and 5s into \(1{,}000\). For \(\dfrac{3}{25}\), multiply by \(2^2\) and you land on \(100\). But \(\dfrac{5}{12}\) is stuck. Its denominator holds a 3, and no matter how many 2s or 5s you multiply on, that 3 never disappears, so the denominator can never become a power of ten. The fraction that cannot terminate is \(\dfrac{5}{12}\), and its denominator is \(\boxed{12}\).

blocks.x: The termination test. Put the fraction in lowest terms and factor the denominator. If the only primes there are $2$s and $5$s, you can feed it the missing $2$s or $5$s to grow it into a power of ten, so its decimal stops. But the moment any other prime sneaks in — a $3$, a $7$, an $11$ — no power of ten will ever fit and the decimal runs forever. The reason is one idea in three coats: a fraction and its decimal are the same number, and the decimal of $\tfrac{a}{b}$ is just $a\div b$, so scales to a power of ten, bottom is only $2$s and $5$s, and the division ends all describe the same situation. $2$ and $5$ are the gatekeepers of a clean decimal name.

blocks.cap: Factor the denominator in lowest terms. If you see only 2s and 5s, it terminates, because you can always add enough of each to climb to a power of ten. Here \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\) just needs two more 5s to reach \(1{,}000 = 2^3 \times 5^3\). But \(6 = 2 \times 3\) carries a stray \(3\), and no pile of extra 2s and 5s can ever absorb it, so that fraction never stops. We will see what it does instead in 5.5.

blocks.x: Babylonians counted in base \(60 = 2^2 \times 3 \times 5\), so \(\tfrac{1}{3} = \tfrac{20}{60}\) was a clean finite number for them. Whether a decimal terminates depends on the base, not the fraction.
A board is \(5.75\) feet. The carpenter marks off eighths of a foot. Convert \(5.75\) to a fraction with denominator \(8\). How many eighths of a foot are in \(5.75\)?
hints: The decimal \(5.75\) and the fraction it equals are the same number wearing different clothes. Turn \(5.75\) into a fraction first, then think about how to rewrite that fraction so its bottom number is \(8\). Once the bottom is \(8\), the top tells you how many eighths.
Write \(5.75\) as \(\frac{575}{100}\), which reduces to \(\frac{23}{4}\). To rename \(\frac{23}{4}\) with a bottom of \(8\), multiply the top and bottom by \(2\). Read off the new top number.
blocks.sol: Asking how many eighths are in a number is just choosing a coat whose bottom is \(8\) and then reading off the top. So the whole job is to dress \(5.75\) as a fraction over \(8\). Start by writing the decimal as a fraction. The \(75\) sits in the hundredths place, so \(5.75 = \frac{575}{100}\). That fraction is bulky, so reduce it. Both \(575\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(25\), and \(575 \div 25 = 23\) while \(100 \div 25 = 4\), which gives \(\frac{23}{4}\). Now rename \(\frac{23}{4}\) so its bottom is \(8\). Since \(4 \times 2 = 8\), multiply the top and bottom by the same \(2\), $$\frac{23}{4} = \frac{23 \times 2}{4 \times 2} = \frac{46}{8}.$$ The bottom is now \(8\), so each piece is one eighth, and the top simply counts how many of them there are. The marks really do land exactly, with \(46\) of them filling the board with nothing left over. There are \(\boxed{46}\) eighths in \(5.75\).

blocks.x: Counting units by renaming. When a question asks how many of some unit fit inside a number — how many eighths, how many fifths — it is really asking you to rewrite that number as a fraction whose bottom is that unit; then the numerator on top is the count. To hunt for eighths, steer the bottom to $8$; to hunt for fifths, steer it to $5$. So knowing both names of a number is not only for converting back and forth — it is a way to count and compare, since you pick the name with the bottom you want and read the answer straight off the top.
Find the reciprocal of \(2.5\) and write it as a decimal. Convert \(2.5\) to a fraction, flip it, then convert back to a decimal.
hints: Start by putting on the fraction coat. The digits of \(2.5\) are \(25\), and there is one place after the point, so \(2.5 = \frac{25}{10}\). Now reduce that to lowest terms before you flip, since a tidy fraction is much easier to turn over.

In lowest terms \(2.5 = \frac{5}{2}\). Flipping a fraction means swapping the top and bottom, so the reciprocal of \(\frac{5}{2}\) is \(\frac{2}{5}\). Now you just need \(\frac{2}{5}\) as a decimal. Scale the bottom up to \(10\) by multiplying top and bottom by \(2\).
blocks.sol: Take the round trip one step at a time. Step 1, decimal to fraction. The number \(2.5\) has one digit after the point, so it is twenty five tenths, \(2.5 = \frac{25}{10}\). Both \(25\) and \(10\) share a factor of \(5\), so divide them out to get the lowest terms version, \(\frac{25}{10} = \frac{5}{2}\). Step 2, flip for the reciprocal. Taking a reciprocal just swaps the top and bottom of a fraction, exactly like Chapter 4. So the reciprocal of \(\frac{5}{2}\) is \(\frac{2}{5}\). Notice how much friendlier this flip is on the fraction than it would be on the messy decimal. Step 3, fraction to decimal. Turn \(\frac{2}{5}\) into tenths by multiplying the top and bottom by \(2\), which gives \(\frac{2}{5} = \frac{4}{10}\), and four tenths written as a decimal is \(0.4\). Look back at the whole journey. You walked in carrying a decimal, slipped into a fraction so the flip was clean, then walked back out as a decimal, a full round trip through both names. As a quick check, \(2.5 \times 0.4 = 1\), which is exactly what a number and its reciprocal must multiply to. \(\boxed{0.4}\)

blocks.x: A calculator shows exactly \(0.84\). The divisor was less than \(30\) and the division was already in simplest form. Reduce \(\tfrac{84}{100}\) and read off the dividend.
hints: The screen shows \(0.84\), which is just another way of writing \(\tfrac{84}{100}\). Strip that fraction all the way down to lowest terms and study the denominator you land on. That denominator has to match the real divisor, since the student says nothing could be cancelled anymore.

Once \(\tfrac{84}{100}\) is in lowest terms its denominator is the smallest whole number you could have divided by. The clue says the actual divisor is under \(30\), so see whether that lowest-terms denominator fits. If it does, it must BE the divisor, and the matching number on top is your dividend.
blocks.sol: Start with what the screen actually says. A reading of \(0.84\) means eighty-four hundredths, so \(0.84 = \tfrac{84}{100}\). That is not the original division yet, because \(84\) and \(100\) still share a factor. Their \(\gcd\) is \(4\), so divide top and bottom by \(4\): $$\frac{84}{100} = \frac{84 \div 4}{100 \div 4} = \frac{21}{25}.$$ Now \(21\) and \(25\) have nothing left in common, so \(\tfrac{21}{25}\) is in lowest terms. The student said their numbers were already in simplest shape before dividing, so the divisor they typed had to reduce to this \(25\). The options would be \(25\) itself, or a multiple like \(50\) or \(75\). But the clue pins the divisor under \(30\), and the only one of those below \(30\) is \(25\). That nails the divisor to \(25\) and the dividend on top to \(21\). One last check explains why the screen could show a clean ending decimal at all. The lowest-terms denominator is \(25 = 5 \times 5\), built from nothing but \(5\)s, so the decimal terminates instead of running forever. Everything lines up. The number that was sitting on top is \(\boxed{21}\).

review.x: A sprint timer showed \(0.3\) of a second. Write \(0.3\) as a fraction over a power of ten.
hints: The decimal \(0.3\) ends in the tenths place. Whatever place the last digit lives in tells you the denominator, so the bottom of your fraction is \(10\).
Put the digit you read, which is \(3\), over that denominator of \(10\). Then check whether \(3\) and \(10\) share any common factor.
review.sol: Read the decimal by its last place. The \(3\) sits one spot to the right of the point, which is the tenths place, so \(0.3\) means three tenths. Writing that over a power of ten gives \(0.3 = \frac{3}{10}\). Now ask whether it can be reduced. The only factors of \(3\) are \(1\) and \(3\), and \(10 = 2 \times 5\) shares neither, so \(\gcd(3,10) = 1\) and the fraction is already in lowest terms. The answer is \(\boxed{3/10}\).

review.x: A bottle is labeled \(0.6\) full. Write \(0.6\) as a fraction in simplest form.
hints: The 6 in \(0.6\) sits in the tenths place, so read the decimal out loud as a number of tenths.
Once you have \(\tfrac{6}{10}\), look for a number that divides both 6 and 10, then divide top and bottom by it.
review.sol: Start by reading the decimal place by place. The single digit after the point is in the tenths place, so \(0.6\) means six tenths, which is \(\frac{6}{10}\). That is already a fraction, but the machine wants simplest form, so check whether the top and bottom share a factor. Both 6 and 10 are even, and in fact \(\gcd(6,10)=2\). Divide top and bottom by 2: $$\frac{6}{10}=\frac{6\div 2}{10\div 2}=\frac{3}{5}.$$ Now 3 and 5 share no common factor larger than 1, so this is as simple as it gets. The bottle is \(\boxed{3/5}\) full.

review.x: \(0.85\) of students voted to keep the lunch menu. Write \(0.85\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The digits 85 sit in the hundredths place, so \(0.85\) means 85 out of 100. Start by writing \(\frac{85}{100}\).
Now reduce. Find the greatest common divisor of 85 and 100. Both end in 0 or 5, so they share a factor of 5. Divide top and bottom by it.
review.sol:
The last digit of \(0.85\) lands in the hundredths place, so the decimal reads as 85 hundredths. That gives the starting fraction \(\frac{85}{100}\).

Next, simplify. Look for the largest number that divides both 85 and 100. Since \(85 = 5 \times 17\) and \(100 = 5 \times 20\), the greatest common divisor is \(5\). Divide the top and bottom by \(5\):

$$\frac{85}{100} = \frac{85 \div 5}{100 \div 5} = \frac{17}{20}.$$

Now 17 is prime and does not divide 20, so the fraction cannot shrink any further. The poll result in simplest form is \(\boxed{17/20}\).

review.x: A drill bit is stamped \(0.375\) of an inch. Start from \(\tfrac{375}{1{,}000}\) and reduce. Write \(0.375\) in simplest form as a fraction.
hints: The decimal stops at the thousandths place, so the starting fraction is \(\tfrac{375}{1{,}000}\). Now look for a number that divides both the top and the bottom.
Both \(375\) and \(1{,}000\) are multiples of \(125\). Dividing top and bottom by \(125\) finishes the job in one move.
review.sol: The last digit of \(0.375\) sits in the thousandths place, so the place value tells you the denominator. That gives \(0.375 = \tfrac{375}{1{,}000}\). Now reduce. The greatest common divisor of \(375\) and \(1{,}000\) is \(125\), since \(375 = 125 \times 3\) and \(1{,}000 = 125 \times 8\). Dividing both parts by \(125\) gives \(\tfrac{375 \div 125}{1{,}000 \div 125} = \tfrac{3}{8}\). A quick check confirms it, \(3 \div 8 = 0.375\), right back where you started. So the drawer gets labeled \(\boxed{3/8}\).

review.x: Convert \(-2.6\) to a single improper fraction in simplest form, keeping the sign. Write your answer as one fraction.
hints: Set the sign aside for a moment and just convert the size, \(2.6\). The \(6\) sits in the tenths place, so \(2.6\) means \(2\) wholes and \(6\) tenths.
Turn \(2.6\) into a single fraction by writing it over its place value, \(\frac{26}{10}\), then reduce. After you simplify, write the minus sign back in front.
review.sol: The minus sign just rides along, so first handle the size \(2.6\) and reattach the sign at the end. The last digit of \(2.6\) is in the tenths place, which tells you the denominator is \(10\). That gives \(2.6 = \frac{26}{10}\). Now simplify. Both \(26\) and \(10\) share a factor of \(2\), since \(\gcd(26,10)=2\), so \(\frac{26}{10} = \frac{26 \div 2}{10 \div 2} = \frac{13}{5}\). That fraction is already improper and in lowest terms, because \(13\) is prime and does not divide \(5\). Finally, bring the negative sign back to the front. The elevation change as a single improper fraction is \(\boxed{-13/5}\). A quick sanity check, \(\frac{13}{5} = 2.6\), and the sign matches the original reading.

review.x: A rain gauge reads \(\tfrac{73}{100}\) of a liter. The denominator is already a power of ten. Write \(\tfrac{73}{100}\) as a decimal.
hints: A denominator of \(100\) means hundredths, so the numerator fills the first two spots after the decimal point.
Place \(73\) in the tenths and hundredths positions. There are no whole liters yet, so a \(0\) sits in front of the point.
review.sol: When the denominator is a power of ten, you do not have to divide at all. You just read the numerator into the right decimal places. The denominator here is \(100\), which counts hundredths, so the value goes out to the second place after the point. Drop the \(73\) into the tenths and hundredths spots, and put a \(0\) in front since there are no whole liters. That gives \(\tfrac{73}{100} = 0.73\). You can sanity check it the other way too, since \(0.73\) read aloud is "seventy-three hundredths", which is exactly \(\tfrac{73}{100}\). So the rain gauge holds \(\boxed{0.73}\) of a liter.

review.x: A crew has paved \(\tfrac{4}{5}\) of a trail. Double the numerator and denominator to reach a denominator of \(10\). Write \(\tfrac{4}{5}\) as a decimal.
hints: You want a denominator of 10 because tenths turn straight into a one-digit decimal. What do you multiply 5 by to get 10?
Multiply the top and the bottom by the same number, 2, so the value does not change. That gives you eight tenths, and eight tenths written as a decimal is 0.something.
review.sol: The fastest route to a decimal here is to land on a denominator of \(10\), because a number of tenths is exactly what the first decimal place records. Since \(5 \times 2 = 10\), multiply the top and bottom by the same \(2\) so the fraction keeps its value. \(\frac{4}{5} = \frac{4 \times 2}{5 \times 2} = \frac{8}{10}\). Now \(\frac{8}{10}\) means eight tenths, and eight tenths sits in the first place after the decimal point, so it is \(0.8\). You can sanity check it the other way too, since \(0.8 = \frac{8}{10} = \frac{4}{5}\). The conversion terminates after a single digit, which makes sense because the denominator \(5\) divides evenly into a power of ten. \(\boxed{0.8}\)

review.x: A jar is \(\tfrac{13}{20}\) full. Multiply top and bottom by \(5\) to get a denominator of \(100\), then read the decimal. Write \(\tfrac{13}{20}\) as a decimal.
hints: A decimal is just a fraction whose denominator is a power of ten. Can you turn the 20 on the bottom into 100 without changing the value of the fraction?
Multiply the top and the bottom by the same number, 5. That keeps the fraction equal but renames it with a denominator of 100, which reads straight off as hundredths.
review.sol: Decimals are secretly fractions whose denominators are powers of ten, so the move is to rename \(\tfrac{13}{20}\) with a denominator that is a power of ten. Since \(20 \times 5 = 100\), multiply the top and bottom by the same \(5\) so the value stays the same. $$\frac{13}{20} = \frac{13 \times 5}{20 \times 5} = \frac{65}{100}$$ A fraction over \(100\) is read as hundredths, and \(\tfrac{65}{100}\) is sixty-five hundredths, which is written \(0.65\). You can sanity check it the long-division way too, since \(13 \div 20 = 0.65\), a terminating decimal because \(20 = 2^2 \times 5\) has only \(2\)s and \(5\)s in its factorization. The jar is \(\boxed{0.65}\) full.

review.x: A gauge block is \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) inch thick. Since \(8=2^3\), supply \(5^3=125\) to reach \(1{,}000\). Write \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) as a decimal.
hints: A power of ten is a product of \(2\)s and \(5\)s in equal numbers. The bottom \(8\) is \(2^3\), three \(2\)s and zero \(5\)s. To reach \(10^3 = 2^3 \times 5^3\) you only need to bring in three \(5\)s.
Three \(5\)s multiplied together is \(5^3 = 125\). Multiply the top and the bottom of \(\tfrac{5}{8}\) by that same \(125\) so the value does not change, then read the new fraction as thousandths.
review.sol:
The trick with the scaling method is to ask what the denominator is missing. Here \(8 = 2^3\), so it carries three \(2\)s and no \(5\)s. A power of ten always pairs its \(2\)s and \(5\)s evenly, and the smallest one that uses three \(2\)s is \(10^3 = 2^3 \times 5^3 = 1{,}000\). So the only thing standing between \(8\) and \(1{,}000\) is three \(5\)s, and \(5^3 = 125\).

Multiply the top and bottom by that same \(125\) so the fraction keeps its value.
$$\frac{5}{8} = \frac{5 \times 125}{8 \times 125} = \frac{625}{1{,}000}.$$

Six hundred twenty-five thousandths slides straight onto the readout as \(0.625\). You can sanity check it the long way too. Start from \(\tfrac{4}{8} = 0.5\) and add one more eighth, \(\tfrac{1}{8} = 0.125\), and \(0.5 + 0.125 = 0.625\), the same answer.

\(\boxed{0.625}\)

review.x: Three fractions in lowest terms: \(\dfrac{7}{8}\), \(\dfrac{4}{15}\), \(\dfrac{9}{40}\). Factor each denominator. Which one has a prime other than \(2\) or \(5\)? Give that denominator.
hints: Break each denominator into its prime factors. \(8 = 2^3\) and \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\). What does \(15\) factor into?
A fraction in lowest terms terminates only when its denominator uses nothing but \(2\)s and \(5\)s. Which denominator sneaks in a different prime?
review.sol: Factor each denominator and check whether it is built only from \(2\)s and \(5\)s. For \(\dfrac{7}{8}\), the denominator is \(8 = 2^3\), all \(2\)s, so it terminates. In fact \(\dfrac{7}{8} = 0.875\). For \(\dfrac{9}{40}\), the denominator is \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\), only \(2\)s and a \(5\), so it terminates too, giving \(\dfrac{9}{40} = 0.225\). For \(\dfrac{4}{15}\), the denominator is \(15 = 3 \times 5\). That hidden \(3\) is not a \(2\) or a \(5\), and since the fraction is already in lowest terms nothing can cancel it away. The \(3\) blocks the decimal from ever stopping, so \(\dfrac{4}{15}\) is the card that runs forever. Its denominator is \(\boxed{15}\).

review.x: The full-power brightness factor is \(1.25\). The low-power factor is its reciprocal. Convert \(1.25\) to a fraction, flip it, then write the reciprocal as a decimal.
hints: First turn \(1.25\) into a fraction. The digits after the point sit in the hundredths place, so \(1.25\) is \(\frac{125}{100}\). Reduce that before you flip it.
Once you have the fraction in lowest terms, flip the top and bottom to get the reciprocal. Then look for a way to rewrite the bottom as a power of ten so the decimal falls out cleanly.
review.sol: Start by writing \(1.25\) as a fraction. The last digit lands in the hundredths place, so \(1.25 = \frac{125}{100}\). Both numbers share a factor of \(25\), and \(\frac{125}{100} = \frac{5}{4}\) in lowest terms. The reciprocal just swaps top and bottom, so the reciprocal of \(\frac{5}{4}\) is \(\frac{4}{5}\). To read \(\frac{4}{5}\) as a decimal, rescale the bottom to a power of ten. Multiply top and bottom by \(2\) to get \(\frac{4 \times 2}{5 \times 2} = \frac{8}{10}\), and \(\frac{8}{10} = 0.8\). A quick check, \(1.25 \times 0.8 = 1\), which is exactly what a reciprocal pair should do. \(\boxed{0.8}\)

review.x: A glaze recipe is \(\tfrac{17}{50}\) cobalt. Since \(50 \times 2 = 100\), multiply top and bottom by \(2\). Write \(\tfrac{17}{50}\) as a decimal.
hints: The denominator is \(50\). What single number do you multiply \(50\) by to land on a power of ten like \(100\)?
Multiply top and bottom by \(2\), giving \(\tfrac{17}{50} = \tfrac{34}{100}\). A denominator of \(100\) means two decimal places.
review.sol: The denominator \(50\) factors as \(2 \times 5^2\), only \(2\)s and \(5\)s, so a clean decimal is waiting. To reach a power of ten, climb from \(50\) to \(100\) by multiplying by \(2\). Multiply the top by the same \(2\) so the value does not change, $$\frac{17}{50} = \frac{17 \times 2}{50 \times 2} = \frac{34}{100}.$$ A denominator of \(100\) means hundredths, so the digits \(34\) sit two places past the point. The glaze is \(\boxed{0.34}\) cobalt.

review.x: A shelf is \(3.25\) feet. Convert to a fraction, reduce to \(\tfrac{13}{4}\), then rename with denominator \(8\). How many eighths of a foot are in \(3.25\)?
hints: Start by writing \(3.25\) as a fraction over a power of ten, then reduce it. Two decimal places means the denominator is \(100\), so begin with \(\frac{325}{100}\).
Once \(3.25 = \frac{13}{4}\), ask what you multiply \(4\) by to get \(8\). Whatever you do to the bottom, do to the top, and the number on top is your count of eighths.
review.sol:
The shelf length is \(3.25\) feet, and we want to know how many eighths of a foot that is. The trick is that one number can wear two outfits, a decimal outfit and a fraction outfit, and switching to the fraction makes the counting easy.

First write the decimal as a fraction. Two digits sit after the decimal point, so the natural denominator is \(100\):
$$3.25 = \frac{325}{100}.$$

Now reduce it. Both \(325\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(25\), so divide top and bottom by \(25\):
$$\frac{325}{100} = \frac{325 \div 25}{100 \div 25} = \frac{13}{4}.$$

So \(3.25\) is really \(\tfrac{13}{4}\) in its simplest form. We want this same amount written over a denominator of \(8\). Since \(4 \times 2 = 8\), multiply the top and bottom by \(2\):
$$\frac{13}{4} = \frac{13 \times 2}{4 \times 2} = \frac{26}{8}.$$

Renaming a fraction this way does not change its value, it only changes the size of the pieces we count with. The shelf is \(\tfrac{26}{8}\) feet long, which means it holds \(26\) eighths of a foot. A quick check confirms it, \(26 \div 8 = 3.25\), right back where we started.

So there are \(\boxed{26}\) eighths in \(3.25\).

review.x: A calculator shows exactly \(0.65\). The divisor was less than \(25\) and the fraction was in lowest terms. Reduce \(\tfrac{65}{100}\) to find the divisor. What whole number was divided?
hints: Turn the screen reading into a fraction. The decimal \(0.65\) means \(65\) hundredths, so start from \(\tfrac{65}{100}\) and simplify it as far as it will go.
Once the fraction is in lowest terms, its bottom number is the divisor and its top number is the dividend. Check that the divisor really is under \(25\), then read off the dividend.
review.sol: The screen shows a terminating decimal, so it must come from a clean fraction. Read \(0.65\) as sixty-five hundredths, which gives \(\frac{65}{100}\). Now simplify. Both \(65\) and \(100\) share a factor of \(5\), since \(65 = 5 \times 13\) and \(100 = 5 \times 20\). Dividing top and bottom by \(5\) leaves \(\frac{13}{20}\). The numbers \(13\) and \(20\) have no common factor bigger than \(1\), so this fraction is in lowest terms, which matches Maya's second clue. The bottom number of a fraction is what you divide by, and \(20\) is indeed smaller than \(25\), so the first clue checks out too. The top number is what gets divided, so the dividend is \(\boxed{13}\).

blocks.x: A step counter reads \(4{,}732\). You round to \(4{,}700\). Somewhere between \(4{,}700\) and \(4{,}800\) is a halfway mark. Whichever side you land on determines the rounded answer. What exactly does your brain check in that instant?
Round \(4{,}732\) to the nearest hundred. The midpoint between \(4{,}700\) and \(4{,}800\) is \(4{,}750\). Which neighbor is \(4{,}732\) closer to?
hints: Mark the two neighbors \(4{,}700\) and \(4{,}800\) and find the point exactly between them. Then ask which side of that point \(4{,}732\) falls on.
The midpoint is \(4{,}750\). Since \(4{,}732\) is below \(4{,}750\), it is closer to the neighbor on the left, which is \(4{,}700\).
blocks.sol: The two multiples of one hundred hugging \(4{,}732\) are \(4{,}700\) below and \(4{,}800\) above. The midpoint between them is \(4{,}750\), the halfway mark of that stretch. Now compare. Since \(4{,}732\) is less than \(4{,}750\), it sits on the left half of the stretch, nearer to \(4{,}700\). You can check the nearness directly. The gap down to \(4{,}700\) is \(4{,}732 - 4{,}700 = 32\), while the gap up to \(4{,}800\) is \(4{,}800 - 4{,}732 = 68\). A jump of \(32\) is much shorter than a jump of \(68\), so \(4{,}700\) wins. If the step count had landed exactly on \(4{,}750\), the tie would go to the larger neighbor \(4{,}800\), but here there is no tie. The nearest hundred is \(\boxed{4700}\).

blocks.x: A festival shows \(68{,}431\) attendees. Round to the nearest thousand. The deciding digit is the hundreds digit. What is \(68{,}431\) rounded to the nearest thousand?
hints: The place you are rounding to is the thousands place, the \(8\) in \(68{,}431\). The digit that decides what happens to it is the very next one to the right, the hundreds digit. Circle that digit and ask whether it is big enough to push the thousands place up.

The hundreds digit is \(4\). Since \(4\) is \(4\) or less, the thousands digit stays as it is, it does not tick up. Now drop everything to the right of the thousands place to zero and read off the result.
blocks.sol: First find the place you are aiming for. The nearest thousand lives in the thousands digit, which is the \(8\) in \(68{,}431\). So the answer will be either \(68{,}000\) or the next thousand up, \(69{,}000\). The whole job is deciding which one is closer. The deciding digit is always the one immediately to the right of the place you are rounding to. Here that is the hundreds digit. Reading \(68{,}431\) from the left, the hundreds digit is \(4\). Now the only question that matters, is \(4\) in the "5 or more" group or the "4 or less" group? It is \(4\), so the thousands place stays put and does not climb to \(9\). The last step is the satisfying part. Every digit to the right of the thousands place gets swept down to zero, all at once, no matter what they were. The \(4\), the \(3\), and the \(1\) all become \(0\), giving \(68{,}000\). A quick sanity check confirms it. The two candidates are \(68{,}000\) and \(69{,}000\), and the halfway mark between them is \(68{,}500\). Our count \(68{,}431\) sits below \(68{,}500\), so it really is nearer to \(68{,}000\). $$68{,}431 \approx \boxed{68{,}000}$$

blocks.x: Round \(2{,}950\) to the nearest hundred. The value lands exactly on the midpoint \(2{,}950\), so the tie rule sends it up. The hundreds digit is \(9\), so a carry rolls forward. What is the result?
hints: Find the two nearest hundreds that hug \(2{,}950\), then find the midpoint between them. Which way does the tie rule send you?
You are rounding up from \(2{,}900\) toward \(3{,}000\). The hundreds digit is a 9 and cannot go to 10, so the carry pushes into the thousands. The result is the next clean hundred above \(2{,}950\).
blocks.sol: Mark the two hundreds on either side of \(2{,}950\). The one just below is \(2{,}900\) and the one just above is \(3{,}000\). The midpoint sits halfway between them at \(2{,}950\), which is exactly the number we have. Since we are right on the tie, the larger-value rule sends us to the upper neighbor, \(3{,}000\). Now look at the carry. Rounding up usually means nudging the hundreds digit one step higher, but here that digit is already a 9 and there is no single digit above 9. So the 9 hundreds plus one more hundred makes ten hundreds, which is a whole new thousand. The 2 in the thousands place ticks up to a 3 and the hundreds settle back to 0. That is why \(2{,}950\) does not become \(2{,}1000\), it becomes \(3{,}000\). So \(2{,}950\) rounded to the nearest hundred is \(\boxed{3000}\).

blocks.cap: Every value between \(4{,}700\) and \(4{,}800\) leans toward whichever landmark it is closer to, and the dashed midpoint at \(4{,}750\) is the referee that calls it. Because \(4{,}732\) lands in the bottom half it stays at \(4{,}700\), while \(4{,}768\) lands in the top half and ticks up to \(4{,}800\). The shortcut is the boxed tens digit. A \(3\) means you are under \(50\) and still in the bottom half, a \(6\) means you have reached \(50\) and crossed into the top half, so reading that one digit tells you which side of the referee you are on without walking the whole line.

blocks.x: A 3D printer reads \(7.83\) m of filament remaining. Round to the nearest tenth. The hundredths digit \(3\) decides. What is \(7.83\) rounded to the nearest tenth?
hints: Your two neighbors are \(7.8\) and \(7.9\), and the line between them is at \(7.85\). The only digit that decides which neighbor you get is the hundredths digit, the one right after the tenths place.
The hundredths digit of \(7.83\) is \(3\). Since \(7.83\) is below the midpoint \(7.85\), it stays with the lower neighbor. Keep the tenths digit as it is and drop the rest.
blocks.sol: Rounding to the nearest tenth means choosing whichever multiple of one tenth is closest to \(7.83\). The two candidates are the tenths just below and just above, namely \(7.8\) and \(7.9\), and the fence between them sits exactly halfway, at \(7.85\). So the whole decision comes down to one question. Is \(7.83\) below the fence or above it? The hundredths digit answers that for us, because it measures how far past \(7.8\) we have traveled. Here that digit is \(3\), so we are at \(7.8\) plus three hundredths, which is short of the five hundredths needed to reach \(7.85\). That puts \(7.83\) on the lower side of the midpoint, closer to \(7.8\) than to \(7.9\). We keep the tenths digit exactly as it is and drop everything after it. The half-way rule never even comes up, since \(3\) is not \(5\). \(\boxed{7.8}\)

blocks.x: A tide gauge reads \(3.617\) m. The harbor chart records to the nearest hundredth. The thousandths digit \(7\) decides. Round \(3.617\) to the nearest hundredth.
hints: You only care about which side of the midpoint \(3.615\) the reading falls on. Compare \(3.617\) to \(3.615\). Is it below the midpoint, at the midpoint, or above it?

The digit that decides everything is the one just past the hundredths place, the thousandths digit. Here that digit is \(7\). Anything \(5\) or higher tips you up to the larger neighbor, so keep two digits after the point and bump the hundredths up.
blocks.sol: Rounding to the nearest hundredth means choosing between the two-place values closest to the reading. Those neighbors are \(3.61\) and \(3.62\), and the fence post halfway between them is \(3.615\). The whole decision comes down to which side of that fence the reading lands on. The fast way to see it is to look at the very next digit after the hundredths place, the thousandths digit. In \(3.617\) that digit is \(7\). A thousandths digit of \(5\) or more means the reading is at or above the midpoint, so we move up to the larger neighbor. You can feel it in the distances too. From \(3.617\) down to \(3.61\) is \(0.007\), but up to \(3.62\) is only \(0.003\), so \(3.62\) is genuinely closer. Both ways agree. The harbor chart records the height as \(\boxed{3.62}\) meters.

blocks.x: A lab scale reads \(1.995\) grams. Round to the nearest hundredth. The thousandths digit \(5\) is a tie, so round up. The hundredths digit is \(9\), triggering a carry. What is the result?
hints: The deciding digit is the thousandths digit, the \(5\) in \(1.99\underline{5}\). A \(5\) is the exact halfway case, so round the hundredths place up to the larger value. The only catch is that the digit you are bumping is a \(9\), so think about where it goes when it cannot just become a \(10\).

Bumping the hundredths \(9\) up writes a \(0\) there and carries \(1\) into the tenths place. But the tenths digit is also \(9\), so it becomes \(0\) and carries \(1\) into the ones place, turning \(1\) into \(2\). So \(1.99\) climbs to \(2.00\). Keep both decimal places.
blocks.sol: Start by naming the place we want and the digit that decides it. Rounding to the nearest hundredth means we keep two places after the decimal point, the \(1.99\) part of \(1.995\). The digit just past that, the thousandths digit, is the one that tells us whether to round up or stay put. In \(1.99\underline{5}\) that deciding digit is \(5\). A \(5\) sitting there with nothing but the end behind it is an exact halfway point, and our rule says a tie rounds to the larger value, so we round the hundredths place up. Now the careful part. Rounding up usually just adds \(1\) to the hundredths digit, but that digit is already \(9\). You cannot write a \(10\) in a single slot, so the \(9\) becomes \(0\) and passes a carry of \(1\) to the left, into the tenths place. The tenths digit is also a \(9\), so the same thing happens again. It becomes \(0\) and hands a carry into the ones place, which lifts the \(1\) up to a \(2\). Lining up the carry from right to left, \(1.99\) climbs to \(2.00\). The notebook still records two decimal places, so we keep both zeros and write \(2.00\) rather than just \(2\). Notice what the carry did. It quietly turned a number that started with a \(1\) into one that starts with a \(2\), so the answer actually gained a whole place when the nines toppled like a row of dominoes. $$1.995 \approx \boxed{2.00}$$

blocks.cap: Rounding up a \(9\) works just like a carry in addition. It rolls left through every \(9\) it meets, turning each into a \(0\) and passing a \(1\) onward, which is how \(1.995\) to the nearest hundredth lands on \(2.00\) and not on something starting with \(1.99\).

blocks.x: The rounding rule. To round to a place, look at the single digit just after it, the deciding digit. If it is $5$ or more the place ticks up; if $4$ or less it stays. Either way, every digit after the rounding place drops to zero. You never have to measure the gap to the midpoint, because that one digit already tells you which half you are in. When the deciding digit is exactly $5$ with nothing but zeros behind it, the value sits dead on the midpoint, a tie, and this course sends every tie to the larger value. One wrinkle: if the place you are bumping is a $9$, it cannot climb, so it rolls over to $0$ and carries $1$ into the next place left, exactly like carrying in addition, and if that carry runs off the front the number gains a whole digit, the way $9.96$ rounds to $10.0$.
A freezer reads \(-83\) degrees. Round to the nearest ten. The neighbors are \(-90\) and \(-80\). Measure each gap and pick the closer one. What is the result?
hints: Find the gap from \(-83\) to each neighbor. How far is it left to \(-90\), and how far right to \(-80\)? The shorter trip wins.
From \(-83\) it is \(7\) steps left to reach \(-90\), but only \(3\) steps right to reach \(-80\). The nearer multiple of ten is \(-80\).
blocks.sol: Start by naming the two multiples of ten that hug \(-83\). To its left sits \(-90\), and to its right sits \(-80\). Rounding to the nearest ten just asks which of these two the number is closer to. Now measure each gap. From \(-83\) over to \(-90\) is a jump of \(7\), since \(-83-(-90)=7\). From \(-83\) over to \(-80\) is a jump of only \(3\), since \(-80-(-83)=3\). The trip to the right is much shorter, so \(-83\) is nearer to \(-80\). This is the same midpoint reasoning you use with positive numbers. The halfway point here is \(-85\), and \(-83\) sits to the right of that midpoint, on the side closer to \(-80\). Notice that \(-80\) is the larger of the two values, because it lies further right on the number line, and we landed there simply by being closer, not by any special rule. So the technician records \(\boxed{-80}\) degrees.

blocks.x: A thermostat reads \(-2.35\) degrees. Round to the nearest tenth. \(-2.35\) is the exact midpoint between \(-2.4\) and \(-2.3\). The tie rule rounds to the larger value. What does the display show?
hints: Forget the minus sign for a second and just picture the number line. Mark \(-2.4\) on the left and \(-2.3\) on the right, then drop \(-2.35\) in between. Is it closer to one of them, or stuck dead in the middle?

It is a perfect tie, so use the rule, round to the larger value. "Larger" means farther to the right on the number line. Between \(-2.4\) and \(-2.3\), the one on the right is \(-2.3\), so that is your answer.
blocks.sol: Start by finding the two tenths that \(-2.35\) sits between. Going down to the next tenth gives \(-2.4\), and going up to the next tenth gives \(-2.3\). On the number line \(-2.4\) is on the left and \(-2.3\) is on the right, since numbers grow as you move right. Now check the distances. From \(-2.35\) to \(-2.4\) is \(0.05\), and from \(-2.35\) to \(-2.3\) is also \(0.05\). The gaps match, so \(-2.35\) is the exact midpoint, a true tie. Ties do not get to pick "closer," so the one rule takes over, round to the larger value. Here is the careful part. With negatives, "larger" still means farther to the right on the number line, and the value farther right is \(-2.3\). It can feel backwards because \(2.3\) looks smaller than \(2.4\), but once you attach the minus signs, \(-2.3\) is the bigger number. So we round up toward zero, not away from it. The rounded reading is \(\boxed{-2.3}\).

blocks.x: Rounding negatives. A negative number is not a special case with its own rulebook. It rounds to the nearer multiple exactly like a positive one, and the only thing to watch is what larger means down here. Larger always means farther to the right on the number line, so between two negatives the larger one is the one closer to zero. When a negative value lands exactly on a midpoint, the same round-to-the-larger tie rule sends you toward zero, which is why $-2.35$ rounds to $-2.3$ and not $-2.4$. Same midpoint, same tie rule, just keep your left and right straight.
Three crates weigh \(487\), \(213\), and \(356\) kg. Round each to the nearest hundred, then add the rounded values. What is your estimated total?
hints: Handle one crate at a time. Look at the part of each weight after the hundreds and decide whether it pushes the number up to the next hundred or leaves it at the hundred below.
Round each one. \(487\) is past the halfway mark so it becomes \(500\), \(213\) is below halfway so it becomes \(200\), and \(356\) is past halfway so it becomes \(400\). Now add \(500 + 200 + 400\).
blocks.sol: Round each crate to the nearest hundred first. For \(487\), the part after the hundreds is \(87\), which is past \(50\), so it rounds up to \(500\). For \(213\), the leftover is \(13\), which is below \(50\), so it stays at \(200\). For \(356\), the leftover is \(56\), which is past \(50\), so it rounds up to \(400\). Now the sum is easy because every number ends in two zeros. Add the hundreds, \(5 + 2 + 4 = 11\), so the total is \(1100\). That is \(500 + 200 + 400 = 1100\). Notice how much friendlier these numbers are than the originals. The exact total happens to be \(1056\), so the estimate of \(1100\) is close enough to tell you instantly whether you are nudging the van's limit, and you never had to line up a single column. \(\boxed{1100}\)

blocks.x: A field-trip invoice lists \(29\) students at \(6.10\) dollars each, printing \(1{,}769\) dollars. Round each to a friendly value and multiply. What is your quick estimate of \(29 \times 6.10\)?
hints: You are not finding the exact total here, you are building a quick comparison number. Replace each part with a friendly nearby value first. What is \(29\) rounded to the nearest ten, and what is \(6.10\) rounded to the nearest whole dollar?

Rounding \(29\) to the nearest ten gives \(30\), since \(29\) is much closer to \(30\) than to \(20\). Rounding \(6.10\) to the nearest whole dollar gives \(6\), since \(6.10\) is just past \(6\). Now multiply \(30 \times 6\).
blocks.sol: Estimating works by swapping the messy parts for the nearest easy numbers, multiplying those, and trusting the result to land in the right neighborhood. Start with the count of students. Rounding \(29\) to the nearest ten means asking whether it is closer to \(20\) or to \(30\). It sits one step below \(30\) and nine steps above \(20\), so it rounds up to \(30\). Next round the price. Rounding \(6.10\) to the nearest whole dollar means asking whether it is closer to \(6\) or to \(7\). The ten cents barely lifts it off \(6\), nowhere near the halfway mark of \(6.50\), so it rounds down to \(6\). Now multiply the friendly parts, \(30 \times 6 = 180\). That is your ballpark. Here is the payoff. The printed total claims \(1{,}769\), but your honest estimate is only \(180\), and \(1{,}769\) is about ten times bigger. A gap that size is the unmistakable signature of a whole-digit slip, almost certainly a decimal point that drifted one spot to the right. (The exact total is actually \(29 \times 6.10 = 176.90\), comfortably close to your \(180\), which confirms the printed figure is the wrong one.) The number you would compare against \(1{,}769\) is \(\boxed{180}\).

blocks.x: Estimation as an error-check. Round each piece to a friendly value before you combine them, and you get a quick ballpark close enough to catch a real mistake. Because it is only a ballpark, write it with $\approx$, like $29 \times 6.10 \approx 180$, never an equals sign. The payoff is speed with a safety net: when a printed total like $1{,}769$ lands ten times away from your estimate of about $180$, you know something is wrong before doing any careful work. Rounding trades a little accuracy for a lot of speed, which is exactly what makes it good for checking answers and for mental math.
In 1856 surveyors measured Everest and got exactly \(29{,}000\) feet. Worried a round number would look like a guess, they published \(29{,}002\) feet, inventing two feet to appear more precise.
A gauge is rounded to the nearest tenth and reports \(4.3\) mm. The original reading was stored to two decimal places. What is the largest two-decimal value that still rounds to \(4.3\)?
hints: The reported \(4.3\) covers everything from \(4.25\) up to but not including \(4.35\). You only need the values in that stretch that have exactly two decimal places, so start at the top end and step downward.

Check \(4.35\) first. It sits exactly halfway between \(4.3\) and \(4.4\), so by the larger-value rule it rounds up to \(4.4\), not \(4.3\). That means \(4.35\) is out. Step down one hundredth to \(4.34\) and round it to the nearest tenth to see where it lands.
blocks.sol: A reported \(4.3\) is shorthand for an interval. Every true reading that rounds to \(4.3\) at the tenths place lives between the midpoint below it and the midpoint above it, and those midpoints are \(4.25\) and \(4.35\). The lower end \(4.25\) is exactly halfway between \(4.2\) and \(4.3\), so by the larger-value rule it rounds up into \(4.3\) and is included. The upper end \(4.35\) is exactly halfway between \(4.3\) and \(4.4\), so by that same rule it jumps up to \(4.4\) and is shut out. So the readings that report as \(4.3\) are exactly those with \(4.25 \le \text{value} < 4.35\). We want the largest one that has only two decimal places. The top of the interval, \(4.35\), is not allowed because it rounds to \(4.4\). Step down one hundredth to \(4.34\). Its hundredths digit is \(4\), which is below \(5\), so it rounds down to \(4.3\). It works, and nothing between \(4.34\) and \(4.35\) has just two decimal places, so this is the ceiling. The largest two-decimal reading that still reports as \(4.3\) is \(\boxed{4.34}\).

blocks.cap: The reported \(4.3\) stands for the whole shaded interval from \(4.25\) up to just below \(4.35\). The closed dot at \(4.25\) is in, the hollow dot at \(4.35\) is out, and the star marks \(4.34\) as the largest two-decimal value that still lands here. This is why you can name the boundaries of the original but never recover the exact value, the information fell away when it was rounded.

blocks.x: Rounding is one-way. The moment you round, you trade one exact number for a tidier one and the original is gone for good. A rounded $4.3$ no longer stands for a single value; it stands for a whole stretch, everything from the lower midpoint $4.25$ up to just under the upper midpoint $4.35$, since every original in that band rounds back to $4.3$. That is why rounding is a one-way street: many different originals funnel down to the same rounded value, so you can never run it backward. The most you can ever say in reverse is name the two boundaries the original had to live between.
A price rounds to \(7\) at the nearest dollar, to \(6.8\) at the nearest tenth, and its hundredths digit is \(0\). Exactly one price to the cent satisfies all three. What is it?
hints: Turn each clue into an interval. Rounding to the nearest whole dollar gives \(7\), so the price runs from \(6.5\) up to just under \(7.5\). Rounding to the nearest tenth gives \(6.8\), so the price runs from \(6.75\) up to just under \(6.85\). The price must sit inside both ranges at once, which means inside the tighter one.

The overlap of the two ranges is \(6.75\) up to just under \(6.85\). Now list the prices in that band whose hundredths digit is \(0\). The candidates ending in zero cents are \(6.80\). Check the only one that lands in the band.
blocks.sol: The trick is to read each clue as a range and then hunt for the single point that lives in all of them. Start with clue one. Rounding to the nearest whole dollar gives \(7\), and with the half-way-up rule the prices that round to \(7\) are \(6.5\) up to just under \(7.5\). So far the price is somewhere in \(6.5 \le p < 7.5\). Now clue two. Rounding to the nearest tenth gives \(6.8\), so the prices that round to \(6.8\) are \(6.75\) up to just under \(6.85\), that is \(6.75 \le p < 6.85\). Notice this second range sits entirely inside the first one, so the two clues together pin the price to \(6.75 \le p < 6.85\). Now bring in clue three. The price is written to the cent, and its hundredths digit is \(0\). The prices to the cent inside the band \(6.75\) up to just under \(6.85\) are \(6.75, 6.76, 6.77, \dots, 6.84\). The only one of those whose last digit is \(0\) is \(6.80\). Quick double check. Rounded to the nearest whole dollar \(6.80\) gives \(7\), since \(6.80\) is past the \(6.5\) tipping point. Rounded to the nearest tenth it gives \(6.8\), since the next digit is \(0\) and we keep the tenth. And its hundredths digit is indeed \(0\). All three clues hold, and no other price to the cent does the same. The price per scoop is \(\boxed{6.80}\) dollars.

review.x: A scoreboard reads \(7{,}284\) fans. The press box reports to the nearest hundred. What is \(7{,}284\) rounded to the nearest hundred?
hints: Find the two multiples of one hundred that hug \(7{,}284\). One is below it and one is above it. What are they?
The midpoint sits halfway between those two multiples. Compare \(7{,}284\) to that midpoint. Is the count below the line or above it?
review.sol: Start by trapping \(7{,}284\) between the nearest multiples of one hundred. Counting up in hundreds, the multiple just below is \(7{,}200\) and the one just above is \(7{,}300\). So the true count lives somewhere on the stretch between them. The midpoint of that stretch is the average of the two endpoints, which is \(\frac{7{,}200 + 7{,}300}{2} = 7{,}250\). That midpoint is the fence. A count below \(7{,}250\) falls back to \(7{,}200\), and a count at or above \(7{,}250\) climbs to \(7{,}300\) (half-way rounds to the larger value). Now place the fans. Since \(7{,}284\) is greater than \(7{,}250\), the count sits on the upper side of the fence, so it rounds up. The press box reports \(\boxed{7300}\) fans.

review.x: A fundraiser total of \(3{,}500\) dollars is to be printed rounded to the nearest thousand. \(3{,}500\) is the exact midpoint. Using the half-way-up rule, what goes on the banner?
hints: The two nearest thousands are \(3{,}000\) and \(4{,}000\). Where does \(3{,}500\) sit relative to those two markers?
It sits exactly halfway, so neither one is closer. When that happens, our rule says to pick the larger of the two thousands.
review.sol: To round \(3{,}500\) to the nearest thousand, first find the two thousands it lives between. Those are \(3{,}000\) below and \(4{,}000\) above. Now measure the distance to each one. From \(3{,}500\) down to \(3{,}000\) is \(500\), and from \(3{,}500\) up to \(4{,}000\) is also \(500\). The gaps are equal, so \(3{,}500\) is not closer to either thousand. It sits at the exact midpoint. Because the distances tie, the usual "closer one wins" idea cannot decide for us. That is exactly when our convention steps in. For a halfway value, we round to the larger thousand, which is \(4{,}000\). So the banner should read \(\boxed{4000}\).

review.x: A scale reads \(6.47\) kg. A recipe records to the nearest tenth. Round \(6.47\) to the nearest tenth.
hints: You are rounding to the tenths place, so look at the next digit to its right, the hundredths digit, to decide what happens.
The hundredths digit is 7. Since 7 is 5 or more, the tenths digit goes up by one.
review.sol: You want the value to the nearest tenth, so picture \(6.47\) sitting between the two nearest tenths, \(6.4\) and \(6.5\). The deciding digit is the one just past the tenths place, the hundredths digit, which is \(7\). The halfway mark between \(6.4\) and \(6.5\) is \(6.45\), and \(6.47\) is past it, so it sits closer to \(6.5\). By the rule that a deciding digit of \(5\) or more sends the tenths digit up, the \(4\) becomes a \(5\). So \(6.47\) rounds to \(\boxed{6.5}\).

review.x: A lap is timed at \(0.382\) seconds. The results sheet keeps two decimal places. Round \(0.382\) to the nearest hundredth.
hints: Rounding to the nearest hundredth means keeping two decimal places, so look at the very next digit to the right, the thousandths digit.
The thousandths digit in \(0.382\) is \(2\). Since \(2\) is less than \(5\), the hundredths digit stays the same and you drop everything after it.
review.sol: Keeping two decimal places means the hundredths place is the last one we get to write, so the deciding digit is the next one to the right. In \(0.382\) the hundredths digit is \(8\) and the thousandths digit is \(2\). Picture \(0.382\) sitting on a number line between the two hundredth marks \(0.38\) and \(0.39\). The exact halfway point is \(0.385\), and \(0.382\) sits below that, much closer to \(0.38\). You can also count the gaps. The distance down to \(0.38\) is only \(0.002\), while the distance up to \(0.39\) is \(0.008\). Since the thousandths digit \(2\) is less than \(5\), we leave the hundredths digit alone and cut off the rest. The lap time recorded on the sheet is \(\boxed{0.38}\) seconds.

review.x: A bus ride takes exactly \(9.5\) minutes. The schedule lists whole minutes only. \(9.5\) is exactly halfway between \(9\) and \(10\). What whole number does the schedule show?
hints: Find the two whole numbers \(9.5\) sits between. How far is it from each one?
It is exactly the same distance from \(9\) and from \(10\), so it is a tie. The half-way rule sends a tie to the larger value.
review.sol: The value \(9.5\) falls between the whole numbers \(9\) and \(10\). Check the distance to each. From \(9\) it is \(9.5 - 9 = 0.5\), and from \(10\) it is \(10 - 9.5 = 0.5\). The distances are equal, so this is a true tie, sitting exactly on the midpoint. When a number lands precisely half-way, our convention is to round to the larger value. The larger of \(9\) and \(10\) is \(10\), so the schedule lists this trip as \(\boxed{10}\) minutes.

review.x: A fitness band reads \(0.65\) battery and rounds to one decimal place. \(0.65\) is exactly halfway between \(0.6\) and \(0.7\). What does the band display?
hints: The two tenths on either side of \(0.65\) are \(0.6\) and \(0.7\). Which one is \(0.65\) closer to?
It is not closer to either one, it sits exactly in the middle. The halfway rule says to round to the larger value.
review.sol: Rounding to the nearest tenth means choosing between the two tenths that surround the number. Here those are \(0.6\) and \(0.7\). The distance from \(0.65\) down to \(0.6\) is \(0.05\), and the distance from \(0.65\) up to \(0.7\) is also \(0.05\). So \(0.65\) is a perfect tie, sitting exactly halfway between them. When a value lands right on the midpoint, our rule is to round to the larger value, which means we go up to \(0.7\). The fitness band shows \(\boxed{0.7}\).

review.x: A caliper reads \(0.396\) cm and the spec sheet keeps two decimal places. The thousandths digit \(6\) rounds the hundredths \(9\) up, triggering a carry. Round \(0.396\) to the nearest hundredth.
hints: The hundredths place is the second digit after the decimal point. In \(0.396\) that digit is the \(9\). To decide whether it stays or climbs, look at the very next digit to its right, the one in the thousandths place.

The thousandths digit is \(6\), which is \(5\) or more, so the hundredths place rounds up. But a \(9\) cannot become a \(10\) on its own. When the \(9\) rounds up it carries, just like \(9 + 1\) in regular addition.
review.sol: Find the hundredths place first. In \(0.396\) the digits after the point are \(3\) tenths, \(9\) hundredths, and \(6\) thousandths, so the hundredths place holds the \(9\). To round it, peek at the digit directly to its right, the thousandths digit, which is \(6\). Since \(6\) is at least \(5\), the hundredths place rounds up. Here is the interesting part. Rounding the \(9\) up does not make a single digit \(10\). Instead the \(9\) turns into \(0\) and carries a \(1\) into the tenths place, so the \(3\) tenths becomes \(4\) tenths. The thousandths digit is dropped. That gives \(0.40\). We keep the trailing zero so the answer still shows the hundredths place the spec sheet asked for. \(\boxed{0.40}\)

review.x: A fuel gauge reads \(8.97\) liters and the trip computer shows one decimal place. The tenths digit is \(9\), so rounding it up triggers a carry into the ones. Round \(8.97\) to the nearest tenth.
hints: The hundredths digit is the deciding digit. It is 7, and 7 is at least 5, so the tenths place rounds up.

Rounding up a tenths digit of 9 turns it into 10 tenths. That is a full one, so carry it into the ones place. The 8 becomes 9 and the tenths digit becomes 0. Keep one decimal place to show you rounded to the nearest tenth.
review.sol: Rounding to the nearest tenth means the tenths place is the last digit we keep, so the hundredths digit is the one that makes the decision. In \(8.97\) the hundredths digit is \(7\). Since \(7 \ge 5\), we round the tenths place up. The tenths digit here is already \(9\), and bumping it up gives \(10\) tenths, which is the same as one whole. So the \(9\) in the tenths place rolls over to \(0\) and carries a \(1\) into the ones place. The \(8\) ones become \(9\) ones. Writing the result with one decimal place to match the nearest tenth, we get \(9.0\). A quick gut check helps too. \(8.97\) sits much closer to \(9.0\) than to \(8.9\), so rounding up is exactly what we expect. \(\boxed{9.0}\)

review.x: A wind-chill of \(-46\) degrees is reported to the nearest ten. The two neighboring tens are \(-50\) and \(-40\), with midpoint \(-45\). Which side is \(-46\) on? Round \(-46\) to the nearest ten.
hints: Place \(-46\) between its neighbors \(-50\) and \(-40\). The halfway mark is \(-45\). Is \(-46\) on the side closer to \(-50\) or closer to \(-40\)?
Count the gaps. From \(-46\) to \(-50\) is \(4\) steps, and from \(-46\) to \(-40\) is \(6\) steps. Pick the multiple of ten with the smaller gap.
review.sol: Start by pinning \(-46\) between the two multiples of ten on either side of it. Those are \(-50\) and \(-40\), and the midpoint between them is \(-45\). Now measure how far \(-46\) is from each neighbor. The distance to \(-50\) is \(\left|-46-(-50)\right| = 4\), and the distance to \(-40\) is \(\left|-46-(-40)\right| = 6\). Since \(4\) is smaller than \(6\), the value \(-46\) is nearer to \(-50\). Another way to see it is that \(-46\) sits to the left of the halfway mark \(-45\), and everything left of the midpoint rounds toward the more leftward multiple. The halfway rule that sends a tie to the larger value never comes into play here, because \(-46\) is not a tie. So \(-46\) rounded to the nearest ten is \(\boxed{-50}\).

review.x: An elevation app shows \(-3.5\) m. The value is exactly halfway between \(-4\) and \(-3\). The larger of those two is closer to zero. Round \(-3.5\) to the nearest whole number.
hints: The two whole-meter choices around \(-3.5\) are \(-4\) and \(-3\). On the number line, which one sits farther to the right?
Half-way values round to the larger number. Between \(-4\) and \(-3\), the larger value is \(-3\), since it is closer to zero.
review.sol: The whole meters on either side of \(-3.5\) are \(-4\) and \(-3\). Since \(-3.5\) sits exactly half-way between them, the usual "closer one wins" test gives no winner, so the half-way rule decides. Our convention is to round a half-way value to the larger of the two choices. With negatives it helps to picture the number line. Moving right means getting larger, and \(-3\) sits to the right of \(-4\), closer to zero, so \(-3\) is the larger value. That makes the rounded elevation \(\boxed{-3}\) meters.

review.x: A freezer probe reads \(-1.25\) degrees. The app rounds to the nearest tenth. \(-1.25\) is an exact tie between \(-1.3\) and \(-1.2\). Round to the larger value. What does the app display?
hints: The tenths place is the first digit after the decimal point. The two nearest tenths around \(-1.25\) are \(-1.3\) and \(-1.2\). Which one is more positive, meaning farther to the right on the number line?

The reading is an exact tie, equally far from both choices. The rule here says a tie rounds to the larger value, and on a number line \(-1.2\) sits to the right of \(-1.3\), so \(-1.2\) is the larger one.
review.sol: Rounding to the nearest tenth means choosing between the two tenths on either side of \(-1.25\). Those are \(-1.3\) and \(-1.2\). Check the distance to each. From \(-1.25\) to \(-1.3\) is \(0.05\), and from \(-1.25\) to \(-1.2\) is also \(0.05\). The reading is the same distance from both, so it is an exact tie. The tie-breaking rule sends a halfway value to the larger, more positive number. On the number line \(-1.2\) lies to the right of \(-1.3\), which makes \(-1.2\) the larger of the two. So the app shows \(\boxed{-1.2}\).

review.x: A concert order lists \(41\) tickets at \(19.80\) dollars each, printing a total of \(81.18\) dollars. Round each to the nearest friendly value and multiply. What is your quick estimate?
hints: Handle each part on its own. For 41, look at the ones digit to decide which ten it sits closest to. For \(19.80\), the cents tell you whether it stays at 19 dollars or climbs to the next whole dollar.
The ones digit of 41 is 1, so 41 rounds down to 40. The cents in \(19.80\) are 80, which is past the halfway mark of 50, so \(19.80\) rounds up to 20. Now multiply \(40 \times 20\).
review.sol: Estimation works by trading the messy numbers for friendly ones, then multiplying. Take the parts separately. The number 41 has a ones digit of 1. Since 1 is below 5, the value leans toward the smaller ten, so \(41\) rounds to \(40\). The price \(19.80\) sits between \(19\) and \(20\) whole dollars. The cents are \(80\), which is more than the halfway point of \(50\), so the price leans toward the larger whole dollar and \(19.80\) rounds to \(20\). Now multiply the two rounded parts. \(40 \times 20 = 800\). So the ballpark total is about \(800\) dollars. That number towers over the printed \(81.18\) dollars, which tells you the receipt is almost certainly wrong, probably missing a digit. The estimate is \(\boxed{800}\).

review.x: Three boxes weigh \(596\), \(207\), and \(398\) grams. Round each to the nearest hundred, then add. What is the estimated total?
hints: Look at each weight on its own and find the nearest hundred. The tens digit tells you which way to go, and a tens digit of 5 or more rounds up to the larger hundred.
Round all three first, then add. You should be working with 600, 200, and 400.
review.sol: Round each weight to the nearest hundred one at a time. For \(596\), the tens digit is \(9\), which is well past halfway, so it rounds up to \(600\). For \(207\), the tens digit is \(0\), so it rounds down to \(200\). For \(398\), the tens digit is \(9\), so it rounds up to \(400\). Now add the friendly numbers, \(600 + 200 + 400 = 1200\). The estimate clears the \(1000\) gram mark with room to spare. \(\boxed{1200}\)

review.x: A rain gauge stores readings to the nearest hundredth but its display shows the nearest tenth, reading \(5.3\) mm. What is the smallest two-decimal stored value that rounds to \(5.3\)?
hints: The display rounds to the nearest tenth. The tenths on either side of the shown value are \(5.2\) and \(5.3\). Where does the boundary between rounding down to \(5.2\) and up to \(5.3\) sit?
The midpoint between \(5.2\) and \(5.3\) is \(5.25\). By the halfway rule, that exact midpoint rounds up to \(5.3\). Anything even a hundredth smaller, like \(5.24\), would round down to \(5.2\).
review.sol: The display rounds the stored value to the nearest tenth and shows \(5.3\). For a value to round to \(5.3\) rather than to the neighbor below, \(5.2\), it has to be at least as large as the point exactly halfway between them. That halfway point is \(\frac{5.2 + 5.3}{2} = 5.25\). By the rule that an exact halfway value rounds up to the larger tenth, \(5.25\) itself rounds to \(5.3\). Going one hundredth lower, \(5.24\) is closer to \(5.2\), so it would round down and the display would read \(5.2\) instead. That makes \(5.25\) the smallest two-decimal stored value the gauge could hold while still showing \(5.3\). The smallest qualifying value is \(\boxed{5.25}\).

blocks.x: \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) can never settle into a stopping decimal because \(3\) is neither \(2\) nor \(5\). So what do its digits do? Are they random, or stuck in a loop? The answer hides inside the remainders of long division.
Long-divide \(1 \div 3\). At each step the remainder decides the next digit. What number is the remainder at the end of every step?
hints: Don't watch the quotient digit, watch what is left over after you subtract. Each step you have some number, you take away the biggest multiple of \(3\) you can, and a small leftover stays behind. Write down that leftover for the first two or three steps and see if it changes.

On step one you compute \(10 - 9 = 1\). On step two you pull down a zero to make \(10\) again, and \(10 - 9 = 1\). The leftover after subtracting is the same single digit every single time. Type that digit.
blocks.sol: Watch the remainder, not the digit, and the mystery dissolves. We are computing \(1 \div 3\). Since \(3\) does not divide \(1\), we place the decimal point and bring down a zero, which makes the running number \(10\). The largest multiple of \(3\) that fits in \(10\) is \(9\), so the quotient digit is \(3\) and the remainder is \(10 - 9 = 1\). Now bring down the next zero. The remainder \(1\) becomes \(10\) once more, the digit is \(3\) again, and \(10 - 9 = 1\) again. Here is the key observation. The new remainder, \(1\), is exactly the number we started this whole process with. So step three must be an identical copy of step two, step four a copy of step three, and so on with no end. The remainder is locked at \(1\), which locks the quotient digit at \(3\), forever. That is precisely why \(\tfrac{1}{3} = 0.\overline{3}\), the bar marking the \(3\) that can never escape. The single number that returns at the end of every step is \(\boxed{1}\).

blocks.x: Long-dividing \(1 \div 7\) cycles through remainders \(1, 3, 2, 6, 4, 5\) before \(1\) returns, giving \(\tfrac{1}{7} = 0.\overline{142857}\). How many digits are in the repeating block?
hints: Only \(0\) through \(6\) can ever be a remainder, and \(0\) would stop the decimal. So while it keeps going, every remainder is one of \(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\). The block repeats once a remainder returns, so the longest a block can run before looping is exactly the number of different remainders that show up.

List the remainders in order until one comes back. They are \(1, 3, 2, 6, 4, 5\), and then \(1\) appears again. Count how many remainders you went through before that first repeat. That count is the length of the repeating block.
blocks.sol: Each step of long division produces a remainder, and that remainder is what decides the next digit. So if a remainder ever repeats, the step after it must produce the same digit and the same next remainder as before, and the pattern is locked into a loop from that point on. The length of the repeating block is just how many steps happen between a remainder and its first return. Track the remainders for \(\tfrac{1}{7}\). Start with \(1\). Bring down a zero to get \(10\), and \(10 \div 7\) gives digit \(1\) with remainder \(3\). Then \(30 \div 7\) gives \(4\) remainder \(2\). Then \(20 \div 7\) gives \(2\) remainder \(6\). Then \(60 \div 7\) gives \(8\) remainder \(4\). Then \(40 \div 7\) gives \(5\) remainder \(5\). Then \(50 \div 7\) gives \(7\) remainder \(1\). The remainder \(1\) is back, the one we started with, so the loop closes here. The remainders we passed through were \(1, 3, 2, 6, 4, 5\), which is six of them, and the digits they produced were \(1, 4, 2, 8, 5, 7\). That gives \(\tfrac{1}{7} = 0.\overline{142857}\), a block of six digits. Notice the block could not have been longer than six, since only the six remainders \(1\) through \(6\) are available before a \(0\) would end it, and here all six get used. The repeating block has \(\boxed{6}\) digits.

blocks.cap: Dividing \(1\) by \(7\), the leftover at each step rides around this ring in the order \(1, 3, 2, 6, 4, 5\), and the quotient digit stamped on each arrow spells \(0.\overline{142857}\). The closing gold arrow from \(5\) back to \(1\) is the whole point. Every leftover is smaller than the divisor, so only finitely many leftovers are possible, and the moment one returns the digits are forced to repeat. The size of the loop is the length of the repeating block, the period. Here that loop holds six remainders, so the period is \(6\). Beside it, \(\tfrac{1}{3}\) loops straight back onto remainder \(1\) at once, a loop of one, giving the single digit block \(0.\overline{3}\).

blocks.x: Why decimals repeat. Long division prints each digit from a remainder, and it stops only when a remainder finally lands on $0$. But every remainder is smaller than the divisor $b$, so at most $b-1$ nonzero values can ever appear. If $0$ never shows up, some remainder must eventually return, and the instant it does, every later step copies an earlier one and the digits lock into a repeating block. That is why $\tfrac{1}{3}=0.\overline{3}$ and $\tfrac{1}{7}=0.\overline{142857}$: the leftovers are trapped in a loop. A fraction is always terminating or repeating, never random.
In \(\tfrac{1}{6} = 0.1\overline{6}\), only the \(6\) repeats. How many digits come before the repeating block begins, meaning how many are not under the bar?
hints: Write out the decimal with the bar in the right place. In \(0.1\overline{6}\), the bar covers the looping part. Everything to the left of where the bar starts is the non-repeating head. Just count those digits.
The repeating block is the \(6\), and the bar starts right at it. The only digit sitting before the bar is the \(1\). So count the digits before the loop begins.
blocks.sol: Long division tells the whole story through its remainders. Dividing \(1\) by \(6\), bring down a zero to get \(10\). Then \(10\div 6 = 1\) remainder \(4\), so the first digit after the point is \(1\). Bring down another zero to get \(40\). Then \(40\div 6 = 6\) remainder \(4\). The remainder is \(4\) again, the same one we already saw, so the process is now stuck in a loop and the digit \(6\) repeats endlessly. The decimal is $$\tfrac{1}{6} = 0.1\overline{6} = 0.16666\ldots$$ The bar sits over the \(6\) only, because the loop does not begin until after that first digit. Why one settling digit and not zero? Look at the denominator. \(6 = 2\times 3\). From 5.3, factors of \(2\) and \(5\) are the part that wants to terminate, and they are what create non-repeating head digits. The single factor of \(2\) here produces exactly one such digit, the \(1\), before the factor of \(3\) takes over and forces the endless \(6\). One \(2\) buys one settling digit. So the digits not under the bar number exactly one. $$\boxed{1}$$

blocks.x: The long division \(1 \div 11\) gives \(\tfrac{1}{11} = 0.\overline{09}\). The bar covers both the \(0\) and the \(9\). How many digits are in the repeating block? Give the period.
hints: The period is just a count. Look at the block sitting under the bar in \(0.\overline{09}\) and count its digits, including the leading zero.
The block is \(09\). Count its digits one at a time. There is a \(0\), and there is a \(9\). That is two digits.
blocks.sol: The period is the length of the repeating block, so the real task is to find that block and then count its digits. The cleanest way is to track remainders during long division, because the moment a remainder repeats, the digits must repeat too. Start with \(1 \div 11\). Since \(1\) is smaller than \(11\), we write \(0.\) and carry a remainder of \(1\). Bring down a zero to make \(10\). Now \(10 \div 11 = 0\) with remainder \(10\), so the first digit after the point is \(0\). Bring down another zero to make \(100\). Now \(100 \div 11 = 9\) with remainder \(1\), so the next digit is \(9\). That remainder \(1\) is exactly the remainder we began with, so the process repeats forever from here. The digits it keeps spitting out are \(0, 9, 0, 9, \dots\), which gives $$\frac{1}{11} = 0.\overline{09}.$$ The bar covers \(09\), and that leading zero is essential. Writing \(0.\overline{9}\) would mean \(0.999\dots\), a completely different number. So the repeating block is \(09\), and counting its digits gives \(0\) and \(9\), which is \(\boxed{2}\) digits. The period of \(\tfrac{1}{11}\) is \(\boxed{2}\).

blocks.x: Long division gives \(\tfrac{1}{12} = 0.08\overline{3}\). What is the repeating block? Type the digit or digits under the bar.
hints: The repeating block is the part that keeps coming back once the digits settle into their loop. The \(0\) and the \(8\) at the front each happen only once, so neither of them is part of the loop. Watch for the moment a remainder shows up a second time, because that is exactly when the repetition kicks in.

Carry the division out to \(0.0833333\ldots\). The remainder \(4\) appears, gives the digit \(3\), and then comes back as the remainder again, so the same step repeats and you keep writing \(3\). Only the \(3\) sits under the bar, so \(\tfrac{1}{12} = 0.08\overline{3}\).
blocks.sol: Long division is really a hunt for a repeated remainder, so let us track the remainders carefully. Dividing \(1\) by \(12\), the first step gives \(10 \div 12 = 0\) remainder \(10\), digit \(0\). The second step gives \(100 \div 12 = 8\) remainder \(4\), digit \(8\). The third step gives \(40 \div 12 = 3\) remainder \(4\). That remainder \(4\) is the very same one we just had, so the division has entered a cycle. From here on we will always be computing \(40 \div 12\) again, writing another \(3\), and handing back remainder \(4\). The \(3\) loops, while the \(0\) and the \(8\) at the front each appeared only once and stay put. So \(\tfrac{1}{12} = 0.08\overline{3}\), where the bar sits over the lone digit \(3\). Here is the satisfying part. The head \(08\) is two digits long, and that is no accident. Since \(12 = 2^2 \times 3\), the two factors of \(2\) force exactly two non-repeating digits at the start, and the leftover factor \(3\) is what generates the looping tail. The block under the bar is a single digit. \(\boxed{3}\)

blocks.x: Which of \(\tfrac{1}{16}\), \(\tfrac{1}{20}\), \(\tfrac{1}{25}\), \(\tfrac{1}{15}\) repeats? Factor each denominator and apply the \(2\)s-and-\(5\)s test. Give the denominator.
hints: Break each denominator into primes. \(16 = 2^4\), \(20 = 2^2 \times 5\), \(25 = 5^2\), and \(15 = 3 \times 5\). The terminating ones use only the "friendly" primes \(2\) and \(5\). Hunt for the denominator that smuggles in a different prime.

Three of the denominators are made purely of \(2\)s and \(5\)s, so those three terminate. Only \(15 = 3 \times 5\) carries a prime that is neither \(2\) nor \(5\). That stray factor of \(3\) is what forces the digits to repeat, so the answer is its denominator.
blocks.sol: The whole question lives in the prime factorizations, so factor all four denominators and never touch long division. \(16 = 2^4\), made of nothing but \(2\)s. \(20 = 2^2 \times 5\), only \(2\)s and a \(5\). \(25 = 5^2\), only \(5\)s. \(15 = 3 \times 5\), and there it is, a \(3\) hiding among the friendly primes. The rule from 5.3, sharpened by this lesson, says a lowest-terms fraction terminates exactly when its denominator is built solely from \(2\)s and \(5\)s. Those are the only primes that divide a power of \(10\), so a denominator of that shape can always be scaled up into a clean power of ten, which is what a terminating decimal really is. A \(3\) can never be turned into a \(10\) by piling on more \(2\)s and \(5\)s, so it has no way to clear out. In the long division for \(\tfrac{1}{15}\) the remainders cycle instead of reaching \(0\), and you get \(\tfrac{1}{15} = 0.0\overline{6}\), where the \(6\) loops without end. Meanwhile the other three stop cold, \(\tfrac{1}{16} = 0.0625\), \(\tfrac{1}{20} = 0.05\), and \(\tfrac{1}{25} = 0.04\). One stray prime was the entire story. The repeating fraction is \(\tfrac{1}{15}\), so the denominator you want is \(\boxed{15}\).
