blocks.cap: The bar sits only over the block that cycles. \(\tfrac17=0.\overline{142857}\) repeats from the first digit (period 6), while \(\tfrac1{12}=0.08\overline{3}\) settles at \(08\) first, then repeats the \(3\).

blocks.x: Read the decimal from the denominator. Factor the denominator of a fraction in lowest terms and you can predict its decimal before dividing. The $2$s and $5$s are the stopping primes, and whichever of those two counts is larger sets the length of a short non-repeating head. Every other prime is a looping prime that builds the repeating tail. So only $2$s and $5$s gives a clean stop, none of them repeats from the very first digit, and a mix gives a head then a loop.

Every fraction produces an endless repeating decimal, but the reverse trip is also possible. The shift-and-subtract trick lines up two copies of the same tail so they cancel, collapsing an infinite decimal into a finite fraction.
Let \(x = 0.\overline{7}\). Multiply by \(10\) to get \(10x = 7.\overline{7}\). The tails of \(7\)s cancel when you subtract: \(9x = 7\). Write \(0.\overline{7}\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: You built two equations, \(x = 0.7777\ldots\) and \(10x = 7.7777\ldots\). Stack the second over the first and subtract straight down. On the left you get \(10x - x\). On the right, the endless \(7\)s line up exactly and wipe each other out, so what whole number is left?
Subtracting gives \(9x = 7\). To get \(x\) by itself, divide both sides by \(9\). That leaves \(x = \tfrac{7}{9}\). Check whether \(7\) and \(9\) share any common factor before you call it done.
blocks.sol:
Start by naming the number. Let \(x = 0.\overline{7}\), so \(x = 0.7777\ldots\) with the \(7\) repeating forever. The reason you cannot just read off a fraction is that the tail never ends, so the trick is to manufacture a second copy of that same tail and line it up.

Multiply both sides by \(10\). One factor of \(10\) shifts the decimal point exactly one place, which is just enough to move past the first \(7\) and expose an identical tail underneath. $$10x = 7.7777\ldots$$ Now write the two facts together and subtract the bottom from the top. $$\begin{array}{r} 10x = 7.7777\ldots \\ -\;\;\;x = 0.7777\ldots \\ \hline 9x = 7.0000\ldots \end{array}$$ Watch what happened on the right. Past the decimal point, \(10x\) had \(7777\ldots\) and \(x\) had \(7777\ldots\), the very same endless string, so digit by digit they cancel to nothing. The infinite part vanishes, and all that survives is \(7 - 0 = 7\). On the left, \(10x - x = 9x\). So the endless decimal has collapsed into the clean statement \(9x = 7\).

Solve it. Divide both sides by \(9\) to get \(x = \tfrac{7}{9}\). Since \(7\) is prime and does not divide \(9\), the fraction is already in lowest terms. And it makes sense as a finale, every infinitely repeating decimal you will ever meet is secretly a fraction in disguise, and the shift-and-subtract move is the key that unlocks it. $$\boxed{7/9}$$

blocks.cap: Shifting by exactly one block length lines the endless tails up perfectly, so when you subtract they erase each other and an ordinary equation is left behind. Here \(10x - x = 7.\overline{7} - 0.\overline{7}\) gives \(9x = 7\), so \(0.\overline{7} = \tfrac{7}{9}\). The same trick scales to any pure repeater. A block of length \(k\) becomes the block written over \(k\) nines, for example \(0.\overline{12} = \tfrac{12}{99}\).

blocks.x: Let \(x = 0.\overline{12}\). Multiply by \(100\) to get \(100x = 12.\overline{12}\). Subtracting gives \(99x = 12\), so \(x = \tfrac{12}{99}\). Write \(0.\overline{12}\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: You already know it begins as \(\tfrac{12}{99}\). Now ask the lowest-terms question. What whole number divides both \(12\) and \(99\) evenly?

Both \(12\) and \(99\) are divisible by \(3\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(3\), which gives \(12 \div 3 = 4\) and \(99 \div 3 = 33\). Check that \(4\) and \(33\) share no common factor, then you are finished.
blocks.sol:
Start by naming the number. Let \(x = 0.\overline{12} = 0.121212\ldots\)

The block has two digits, so shift the decimal point two places by multiplying by \(100\). $$100x = 12.121212\ldots$$ Now line the two equations up and subtract, so the matching infinite tails cancel. $$\begin{array}{r} 100x = 12.121212\ldots \\ -\ \ \ x = \ 0.121212\ldots \\ \hline 99x = 12 \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \end{array}$$ Everything after the decimal point is gone, which is the whole point of shifting by the full length of the block. Solve for \(x\). $$x = \frac{12}{99}$$ This is the block over two nines, exactly the pattern. But \(\tfrac{12}{99}\) is not in lowest terms yet. Both numbers are divisible by \(3\), since \(1+2 = 3\) and \(9+9 = 18\) are each multiples of \(3\). $$\frac{12}{99} = \frac{12 \div 3}{99 \div 3} = \frac{4}{33}$$ Now \(4 = 2 \times 2\) and \(33 = 3 \times 11\) share no common factor, so this is fully reduced. As a sanity check, dividing \(4\) by \(33\) gives \(0.121212\ldots\), right back where we started.

\(0.\overline{12} = \boxed{4/33}\)

blocks.x: Let \(x = 0.\overline{9}\). Multiply by \(10\): \(10x = 9.\overline{9}\). Subtract: \(9x = 9\). Divide by \(9\). What does \(0.\overline{9}\) equal?
hints: You already did the hard part for \(0.\overline{7}\). Write \(x = 0.\overline{9}\) and \(10x = 9.\overline{9}\), then subtract. Trust the cancellation even though the result feels surprising.

Subtracting gives \(10x - x = 9.\overline{9} - 0.\overline{9}\). The infinite tails of nines are identical, so they vanish and leave \(9x = 9\). Now divide both sides by 9 and read off the single whole number you get.
blocks.sol: Set \(x = 0.\overline{9}\), so \(x = 0.9999\dots\) with the nines never stopping. Multiply by 10, which slides the decimal point one place to the right. $$10x = 9.\overline{9} = 9.9999\dots$$ Here is the key observation. Both \(10x\) and \(x\) end in the very same endless string of nines after the decimal point, \(.9999\dots\). They are not nearly the same, they are exactly the same tail. So when you subtract, that whole infinite tail cancels cleanly. $$10x - x = 9.9999\dots - 0.9999\dots = 9$$ On the left, \(10x - x = 9x\), which gives $$9x = 9.$$ Divide both sides by 9. $$x = \frac{9}{9} = 1.$$ Look back at what just happened. Every step was ordinary algebra, the same algebra that sent \(0.\overline{7}\) to \(\tfrac{7}{9}\). Nowhere did you round, drop a piece, or settle for "close enough". The answer is not a number creeping up toward 1 and not a sliver below it. It is 1, on the nose. So \(0.\overline{9}\) and 1 are simply two different names for the same number, and that is a fitting place to close the chapter. $$\boxed{1}$$

blocks.x: Write \(0.41\overline{6}\) as a fraction in lowest terms. Multiply by \(1{,}000\) and by \(100\), subtract to cancel the \(\overline{6}\) tails, then solve and reduce.
hints: You want two shifted copies of \(x\) whose decimal tails are identical, so the subtraction wipes the tail out. The repeating part is \(0.00\overline{6}\), sitting two places in. Try sliding the point so one copy stops just before the loop and the other stops just after one block of it.

Let \(x = 0.41\overline{6}\). Multiply by \(100\) to land just before the loop, \(100x = 41.\overline{6} = 41.666\ldots\). Multiply by \(1{,}000\) to land one block past it, \(1{,}000x = 416.\overline{6} = 416.666\ldots\). Both end in the same \(.666\ldots\) tail. Subtract the smaller from the larger and solve for \(x\), then reduce.
blocks.sol:
Let \(x = 0.41\overline{6} = 0.41666\ldots\). The trouble is the \(41\) up front. It does not repeat, so if we shift to line up tails carelessly we will trap that head inside the subtraction. The fix is to shift past it first, then shift one more block.

Multiply by \(100\) to slide the point right up to the edge of the loop. $$100x = 41.\overline{6} = 41.666\ldots$$ Now everything to the left of the point is settled, and the leftover tail is exactly \(0.666\ldots\).

Multiply the original by \(1{,}000\), which is one extra block further. $$1{,}000x = 416.\overline{6} = 416.666\ldots$$ This tail is also exactly \(0.666\ldots\), the same as before, because the \(6\) loop never changes no matter how far you walk into it.

The two numbers share an identical \(.666\ldots\) tail, so subtracting cancels it cleanly. $$1{,}000x - 100x = 416.666\ldots - 41.666\ldots$$ $$900x = 375$$

Solve for \(x\) and reduce. Both \(375\) and \(900\) share a factor of \(75\), since \(375 = 75 \cdot 5\) and \(900 = 75 \cdot 12\). $$x = \frac{375}{900} = \frac{5}{12}$$

A quick sanity check, \(5 \div 12 = 0.41666\ldots\), which is \(0.41\overline{6}\) exactly. The head shifted out, the tails cancelled, and the whole chapter folds shut. \(\boxed{5/12}\)

blocks.x: Turn a repeater back into a fraction. Shift by exactly one block length and subtract, so the endless tails line up and cancel, leaving a plain equation. A pure repeater becomes its block over a string of $9$s, one $9$ per digit: $0.\overline{7}=\tfrac{7}{9}$, $0.\overline{12}=\tfrac{12}{99}$, $0.\overline{481}=\tfrac{481}{999}$; then reduce. The same move settles a famous case: $0.\overline{9}=\tfrac{9}{9}=1$ exactly, not creeping toward $1$ but already there, since an endless decimal is one fixed number.
In base \(7\), the fraction \(\tfrac{1}{7}\) would simply be \(0.1\), while \(\tfrac{1}{2}\) would repeat. Whether a decimal terminates depends on the base, not the fraction.
\(\tfrac{1}{7} = 0.\overline{142857}\), a block of \(6\) digits. Divide \(50 \div 6\) to find the position within the block. What is the 50th digit after the decimal point?
hints: The block \(142857\) is six digits long, so the pattern resets every \(6\) positions. Every time you pass a full group of \(6\), you are back at the start of the block. So really you just need to know how far past the last full group position \(50\) sits.

Divide \(50\) by \(6\). You get \(8\) full blocks with \(2\) left over. That remainder of \(2\) tells you the \(50\)th digit is the same as the \(2\)nd digit of the block \(142857\). Read off the second digit.
blocks.sol: The decimal is \(\tfrac{1}{7} = 0.\overline{142857}\), so the same block of six digits, \(142857\), repeats forever. Label the spots inside one block by position. The \(1\)st digit is \(1\), the \(2\)nd is \(4\), the \(3\)rd is \(2\), the \(4\)th is \(8\), the \(5\)th is \(5\), and the \(6\)th is \(7\). After that the block starts over, so position \(7\) is \(1\) again, position \(8\) is \(4\), and the cycle keeps turning. The key move is that every group of \(6\) positions returns you to the same place in the block. So to find position \(50\), see how many full groups of \(6\) fit and what is left over. $$50 = 6 \times 8 + 2$$ Eight complete blocks carry you up through position \(48\), which is the end of the eighth block, a \(7\). Then you take \(2\) more steps into a fresh block. Step one lands on \(1\) (position \(49\)) and step two lands on \(4\) (position \(50\)). The remainder \(2\) points straight at the \(2\)nd digit of \(142857\), and that digit is \(4\). No fifty rounds of division needed, just one little division to find your place in the loop. \(\boxed{4}\)

blocks.x: Finding a far digit. Once you know the repeating block and its length, the decimal is settled forever, so any digit is just a question of where its position lands in one trip around the block. Divide the position by the period and read the remainder: remainder $1$ is the first digit of the block, remainder $2$ the second, and remainder $0$ means you stopped on the last digit. No long division is needed beyond finding the period.
Find \(0.\overline{72} - 0.\overline{27}\) as a single fraction in lowest terms. Convert each to a fraction over \(99\), subtract, then reduce.
hints: Each of these has a two-digit repeating block, and you found a shortcut for exactly this. A repeating two-digit block sits over \(99\). So turn each decimal into a fraction with denominator \(99\) before you do anything else.

Once both are written over \(99\), they share a denominator, so you can subtract the top numbers directly and keep the \(99\) underneath. That leaves one fraction. Now reduce it by dividing the top and bottom by their greatest common factor.
blocks.sol: Start by converting each runner with the block-over-nines move you discovered earlier in the chapter. A repeating two-digit block always sits over \(99\), so $$0.\overline{72} = \frac{72}{99}, \qquad 0.\overline{27} = \frac{27}{99}.$$ Here is the satisfying part. Both fractions already share the denominator \(99\), so there is no common-denominator work to do. You just subtract the numerators and keep the \(99\) underneath. $$\frac{72}{99} - \frac{27}{99} = \frac{72 - 27}{99} = \frac{45}{99}.$$ Now reduce all the way down using the greatest-common-factor habit from Chapter 4. Both \(45\) and \(99\) are divisible by \(9\), since \(45 = 9 \times 5\) and \(99 = 9 \times 11\). Dividing top and bottom by \(9\) gives $$\frac{45}{99} = \frac{45 \div 9}{99 \div 9} = \frac{5}{11}.$$ Since \(5\) is prime and does not divide \(11\), this is fully reduced. As a quick sanity check, \(\frac{5}{11}\) is itself the repeating decimal \(0.\overline{45}\), and indeed \(0.727272\dots\) minus \(0.272727\dots\) leaves \(0.454545\dots\) at every place. The first runner beats the second by \(\boxed{5/11}\).

review.x: Long-divide \(2 \div 3\). Each step ends with the same remainder. What single digit repeats forever in the decimal \(0.\overline{6}\)?
hints: Notice that each step in the division leaves the very same remainder of \(2\). When a remainder comes back, the next digit must be the same as the last one, so the decimal just loops.
The decimal is \(0.6666\ldots\), written compactly as \(0.\overline{6}\). The thing under the bar is the part that repeats.
review.sol: Long division is really a remainder machine, and the moment a remainder shows up twice, you know the digits will loop. Start with \(2 \div 3\). Since \(3\) is bigger than \(2\), the whole-number part is \(0\), and we carry a remainder of \(2\) past the decimal point. Now we divide \(20\) by \(3\). Because \(3 \times 6 = 18\), the digit is \(6\) and the leftover is \(20 - 18 = 2\). That leftover of \(2\) is exactly the remainder we began with, so the next step is identical, and so is the one after that. The machine is stuck in a loop, printing a \(6\) on every turn. This gives the endless decimal $$2 \div 3 = 0.6666\ldots = 0.\overline{6}.$$ The bar sits over the block that repeats, and here that block is a single digit. So the digit that goes on forever is \(\boxed{6}\).

review.x: Long-divide \(1 \div 9\). The remainder returns immediately, giving \(\tfrac{1}{9} = 0.\overline{1}\). How many digits are in the repeating block? Give the period.
hints: The repeating block is whatever sits under the bar. Write out \(\tfrac{1}{9}\) as a decimal and look at what is covered.
In \(0.\overline{1}\) the bar sits over a single digit, the 1. Count how many digits are inside that block.
review.sol: Do the long division and watch the remainder. Since \(9\) does not go into \(1\), place a decimal point and bring down a zero to make \(10\). Now \(9\) goes into \(10\) one time, since \(9 \times 1 = 9\), and the remainder is \(10 - 9 = 1\). That remainder of \(1\) is exactly the number we started with, so the next step repeats the same digit, and so does the step after that, with no end. Every step writes a single \(1\). So the decimal is $$\frac{1}{9} = 0.1111\ldots = 0.\overline{1}.$$ The bar covers just one digit, the lone \(1\). A repeating block that is a single digit has a period of one, and \(\tfrac{1}{9}\) is the simplest pure repeater there is. The number of digits in the repeating block is \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: Long-divide \(1 \div 11\). The remainder returns after two digits, giving \(\tfrac{1}{11} = 0.\overline{09}\). Write the repeating block as a digit string, including the leading zero.
hints: The block is the run of digits that repeats forever. You found two digits before the remainder returned to its starting value of \(1\), so the block is two digits long.
The first repeating digit came from \(11\) going into \(10\) zero times, and the second came from \(11\) going into \(100\) nine times. Keep that leading zero, the block is not just \(9\).
review.sol: Long division is really a remainder chase, and the moment a remainder repeats, the whole pattern repeats with it. Start the division \(1\div 11\). The first remainder is \(1\). Attach a zero to get \(10\), and ask how many times \(11\) fits into \(10\). It fits \(0\) times, so the first decimal digit is \(0\) and the remainder stays \(10\). Attach another zero to get \(100\). Now \(11\) fits \(9\) times since \(9\times 11=99\), so the second decimal digit is \(9\) and the new remainder is \(100-99=1\). That remainder of \(1\) is exactly the one we started with, so from here the digits \(0\) then \(9\) will cycle forever. That gives \(\frac{1}{11}=0.0909\ldots=0.\overline{09}\). The repeating block is both digits together, and the leading zero is part of the block, not decoration. So the two-digit repeating block, written as a digit string, is \(\boxed{09}\).

review.x: \(5 \div 6 = 0.8\overline{3}\). The digit \(8\) settles before the loop. How many digits come before the repeating block begins?
hints: Write the decimal out and mark the bar. In \(0.8\overline{3}\), the bar covers only the \(3\). Everything to the left of where the bar starts is the non-repeating head.
Look at the digits after the point one at a time. The \(8\) comes first and never repeats, then the \(3\) repeats forever. Count only the digits that live outside the bar.
review.sol:
Long division shows the whole story. Bring down to get \(50 \div 6\), which is \(8\) with a remainder of \(2\). That \(8\) is the first decimal digit. Now carry the remainder \(2\) forward, so \(20 \div 6\) is \(3\) with a remainder of \(2\) again. Since the remainder is back to \(2\), the same step repeats, so the digit \(3\) loops forever.

That gives \(\frac{5}{6} = 0.8333\ldots = 0.8\overline{3}\). The bar sits over the \(3\) because the \(3\) is the part that repeats. The \(8\) appears once and is never under the bar, so it is the non-repeating head. Counting the digits outside the bar, there is exactly one, the \(8\). 

A decimal like this is called eventually repeating. It settles into its loop after a short run-up rather than right away, and here that run-up is a single digit. \(\boxed{1}\)

review.x: Long-divide \(7 \div 30\) to find \(\tfrac{7}{30} = 0.2\overline{3}\). The \(2\) settles first. What single digit is the repeating block?
hints: Carry out the long division of \(7 \div 30\). Watch the remainders. Once a remainder shows up a second time, the digits from that point on will repeat forever.
The first digit \(2\) settles and is gone for good. After that one digit keeps coming back. That recurring digit is the whole repeating block.
review.sol: Do the long division of \(7 \div 30\) and keep an eye on the remainders. First, \(70 \div 30 = 2\) with remainder \(10\), so the first decimal digit is \(2\). This is the digit that settles, thanks to the factors of \(2\) and \(5\) in \(30\). Next, bring down a zero to get \(100\). Now \(100 \div 30 = 3\) with remainder \(10\). The digit is \(3\), and the remainder is \(10\) again, the same remainder we just had. Once a remainder repeats, the process is locked into a cycle. Every step from here produces another \(3\) with remainder \(10\), over and over. So \(\tfrac{7}{30} = 0.2333\ldots = 0.2\overline{3}\). The \(2\) is the settling digit and the part under the bar is the repeating block. That block is a single digit, so the repeating block is \(\boxed{3}\).

review.x: Let \(x = 0.\overline{5}\). Multiply by \(10\): \(10x = 5.\overline{5}\). Subtract: \(9x = 5\). Write \(0.\overline{5}\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: You have \(9x = 5\) after the subtraction. To get \(x\) by itself, divide both sides by \(9\).
Dividing gives \(x = \tfrac{5}{9}\). Check whether \(5\) and \(9\) share any common factor before you call it final.
review.sol: Start by naming the mystery number. Let \(x = 0.\overline{5} = 0.5555\ldots\). The whole point is that this tail runs on forever with the same digit, and that is exactly what we will use against it. Multiply both sides by \(10\) so every digit shifts one place to the left, which gives \(10x = 5.5555\ldots\). Now line the two equations up. $$10x = 5.5555\ldots$$ $$x = 0.5555\ldots$$ Both numbers end in the identical endless string of fives, so when we subtract the bottom from the top those tails cancel perfectly and nothing is left dangling. On the left \(10x - x = 9x\), and on the right \(5.5555\ldots - 0.5555\ldots = 5\). That leaves the clean little equation \(9x = 5\). Divide both sides by \(9\) to free \(x\), so \(x = \tfrac{5}{9}\). Finally check that the fraction is already in lowest terms. The factors of \(5\) are just \(1\) and \(5\), and \(9 = 3 \times 3\) shares none of them, so nothing reduces. The endless decimal you started with was secretly this exact fraction the whole time, \(\boxed{5/9}\).

review.x: Using the block-over-nines shortcut, \(0.\overline{2}\) has a one-digit block, so it equals \(\tfrac{2}{9}\). Write \(0.\overline{2}\) as a fraction in lowest terms.
hints: The block is one digit long, so the denominator is a single \(9\). Start by writing the block over that nine and you have \(\tfrac{2}{9}\).
Now check whether \(\tfrac{2}{9}\) can shrink. A fraction reduces only when the top and bottom share a common factor bigger than \(1\). What divides both \(2\) and \(9\)?
review.sol: The repeating block is \(2\), and it is a single digit, so it goes over one nine. That gives \(0.\overline{2} = \tfrac{2}{9}\). The last step is to reduce. The factors of \(2\) are \(1\) and \(2\), and the factors of \(9\) are \(1\), \(3\), and \(9\). The only number they share is \(1\), so the fraction is already as small as it gets. To see why the shortcut is honest, let \(x = 0.\overline{2}\). Then \(10x = 2.\overline{2}\), and subtracting gives \(10x - x = 2.\overline{2} - 0.\overline{2}\), so \(9x = 2\) and \(x = \tfrac{2}{9}\). The nine in the denominator is exactly the \(10 - 1\) hiding behind the trick. \(\boxed{2/9}\)

review.x: The decimal \(0.\overline{81}\) equals \(\tfrac{81}{99}\). Reduce \(\tfrac{81}{99}\) to lowest terms. What fraction do you get?
hints: A repeating block of length two always sits over \(99\), which is why \(0.\overline{81}=\dfrac{81}{99}\). Now ask what number divides into both \(81\) and \(99\).
Both \(81\) and \(99\) are multiples of \(9\). Divide the top and the bottom by \(9\) and see what is left.
review.sol: Start by reading the bar. The notation \(0.\overline{81}\) is short for \(0.818181\ldots\), with the two-digit block 81 repeating forever. A repeating block of length two always sits over \(99\), so the fraction you begin with is $$0.\overline{81}=\frac{81}{99}.$$ This value is right, but \(\dfrac{81}{99}\) is not in lowest terms yet, because the top and the bottom share a common factor. The greatest common factor of \(81\) and \(99\) is \(9\), since \(81=9\times 9\) and \(99=9\times 11\). Dividing both parts by \(9\) gives $$\frac{81}{99}=\frac{81\div 9}{99\div 9}=\frac{9}{11}.$$ You can check the result by turning it back into a decimal. Long division of \(9\div 11\) gives \(0.818181\ldots\), the same repeating \(81\), so the conversion is sound. And \(9\) and \(11\) share no common factor other than \(1\), so this is as far as it reduces. The decimal that looked endless was just a neat fraction in disguise, \(\boxed{9/11}\).

review.x: Write \(0.0\overline{45}\) as a fraction in lowest terms. Use \(1{,}000x\) and \(10x\) to cancel the repeating tails, giving \(990x = 45\), then reduce.
hints: The repeating block starts at the second decimal place, not the first. Use the multiplier that jumps past everything once, \(1{,}000x\), and the one that jumps past just the leading zero, \(10x\), so their tails match.
After \(1{,}000x = 45.4545\ldots\) and \(10x = 0.4545\ldots\), subtract to get \(990x = 45\), so \(x = \tfrac{45}{990}\). Now find the greatest common factor of \(45\) and \(990\) and divide it out.
review.sol: Call the number \(x = 0.0\overline{45} = 0.0454545\ldots\). The trouble is that single leading zero, since it appears once and never repeats, so a plain \(100x\) trick will not line the tails up. The fix is to shift twice. Slide the decimal three places to land just before a fresh \(45\), giving \(1{,}000x = 45.454545\ldots\). Slide it one place to clear only the head zero, giving \(10x = 0.454545\ldots\). Both now have the identical repeating tail \(.454545\ldots\). Subtract the smaller from the larger and the tails vanish. $$1{,}000x - 10x = 45.4545\ldots - 0.4545\ldots = 45$$ The left side is \(990x\), so \(990x = 45\) and \(x = \tfrac{45}{990}\). Reduce by the greatest common factor. Both share \(45\), since \(45 = 45 \cdot 1\) and \(990 = 45 \cdot 22\), giving \(\tfrac{45}{990} = \tfrac{1}{22}\). A quick check confirms it, since long division of \(1 \div 22\) gives \(0.0454545\ldots\), the zero once and then \(45\) on a loop. So \(0.0\overline{45} = \boxed{1/22}\).

review.x: Let \(x = 0.\overline{9}\). Multiply by \(10\): \(10x = 9.\overline{9}\). Subtract and solve. What single whole number does \(0.\overline{9}\) equal?
hints: You already have \(9x = 9\). Divide both sides by \(9\) to get \(x\) all by itself.
Both the starting decimal and the answer are the same number \(x\), so whatever \(x\) turns out to be is exactly what \(0.\overline{9}\) is. It is a single whole number, not a fraction.
review.sol: Start by naming the mystery value. Let \(x = 0.\overline{9}\). The bar means the nines repeat forever with no end. Multiplying by \(10\) slides every digit one spot to the left, so $$10x = 9.\overline{9}.$$ The clever part is the subtraction. Line the two equations up and take the difference. $$10x - x = 9.\overline{9} - 0.\overline{9}$$ On the right, the part after the decimal point is an identical endless string of nines in both numbers, so it cancels completely and only the \(9\) in front survives. On the left, \(10x - x = 9x\). That leaves $$9x = 9.$$ Divide both sides by \(9\) to get \(x = 1\). Since \(x\) was \(0.\overline{9}\) the whole time, this says \(0.\overline{9}\) and \(1\) are not close, they are the exact same number written two different ways. A quick second check agrees. From the work in this chapter, \(0.\overline{1} = \tfrac{1}{9}\), so nine of those repeating tenths give \(9 \cdot \tfrac{1}{9} = \tfrac{9}{9} = 1\), and \(9 \cdot 0.\overline{1} = 0.\overline{9}\). Both roads land in the same place. \(\boxed{1}\)

review.x: \(\tfrac{1}{13} = 0.\overline{076923}\), a block of \(6\) digits. Divide \(100 \div 6\) to place the position within the block. What is the 100th digit after the decimal point?
hints: The digits repeat in groups of six, \(076923\). After every full group of six digits you are back at the start, so positions \(6\), \(12\), \(18\), and so on all hold the last digit of the block. Ask where position \(100\) sits relative to a fresh group.
Divide \(100\) by \(6\). You get \(16\) whole groups with a remainder of \(4\), since \(16 \times 6 = 96\) and \(100 - 96 = 4\). So the 100th digit is the 4th digit of the block \(076923\).
review.sol: Start with what the decimal actually is. Doing the long division, \(\tfrac{1}{13} = 0.\overline{076923}\), a pure repeater whose block \(076923\) has length \(6\). So the stream of digits after the decimal point is \(076923\,076923\,076923\dots\), the same six digits over and over with nothing else mixed in. Because the pattern restarts every \(6\) digits, the position of a digit only matters up to multiples of \(6\). Position \(6\) ends the first block, position \(12\) ends the second, and in general every position that is a multiple of \(6\) holds the last digit of the block. To place position \(100\), divide by the block length. \(100 = 6 \times 16 + 4\), because \(6 \times 16 = 96\) and \(100 - 96 = 4\). That means after \(96\) digits we have just finished \(16\) complete blocks and landed exactly on a block boundary, and then we count \(4\) more digits into a fresh copy of \(076923\). Reading that block, the 1st digit is \(0\), the 2nd is \(7\), the 3rd is \(6\), and the 4th is \(9\). So the digit sitting in the 100th place is \(\boxed{9}\). The remainder is the whole trick. The quotient \(16\) just counts how many full blocks went by and never affects which digit you land on, while the remainder \(4\) points straight at the 4th slot of the block. A nice way to feel sure is to notice that position \(96\) is a multiple of \(6\), so it holds the \(6\)th digit \(3\), and then positions \(97, 98, 99, 100\) read off \(0, 7, 6, 9\), landing on \(9\) again.
review.label: r11

review.x: \(\frac{4}{33} = 0.\overline{12}\), a block of \(2\) digits. Divide \(99 \div 2\) to find the position in the block. What is the 99th digit after the decimal point?
hints: The digits repeat with a block of length \(2\). The odd positions \(1, 3, 5, \ldots\) all hold one digit, and the even positions \(2, 4, 6, \ldots\) all hold the other. Which kind of position is \(99\)?
Divide \(99\) by the block length \(2\). The remainder tells you how far into the block you land. A remainder of \(1\) points at the first digit of \(12\).
review.sol: The decimal \(\frac{4}{33} = 0.\overline{12}\) repeats in a block of length \(2\), so every digit is determined by its position within that block. To locate position \(99\), divide by the block length. We get \(99 = 2 \times 49 + 1\), so after \(49\) complete blocks of \(12\) there is a remainder of \(1\). A remainder of \(1\) means we land on the first digit of the block \(12\), which is \(1\). You can sanity check this with the odd-even pattern. Odd positions \(1, 3, 5, \ldots\) always hold the \(1\), and even positions hold the \(2\). Since \(99\) is odd, the digit is the \(1\). $$\frac{4}{33} = 0.\underbrace{12}_{1}\underbrace{12}_{2}\ldots, \qquad 99 \div 2 = 49 \text{ r } 1$$ So the 99th digit is \(\boxed{1}\).

review.x: Factor each denominator: \(8=2^3\), \(40=2^3\times5\), \(50=2\times5^2\), \(24=2^3\times3\). Which denominator contains a prime other than \(2\) or \(5\), forcing its decimal to repeat?
hints: The gatekeeper only lets a fraction terminate if, in lowest terms, the denominator is made of 2s and 5s and nothing else. Three of these pass. Look for the one factorization that hides a different prime.
Scan the four factorizations for a prime that is not 2 and not 5. Only \(24 = 2^3 \times 3\) carries a 3, so that 3 is what forces the repeat.
review.sol: The gatekeeper test says a fraction in lowest terms gives a terminating decimal exactly when its denominator, once fully factored, contains only the primes 2 and 5. Any other prime down there means the decimal repeats forever. So I do not need to divide at all. I just read the prime factorizations. \(8 = 2^3\), all 2s, so \(\tfrac{1}{8}\) terminates. \(40 = 2^3 \times 5\), only 2s and 5s, so \(\tfrac{1}{40}\) terminates. \(50 = 2 \times 5^2\), again only 2s and 5s, so \(\tfrac{1}{50}\) terminates. That leaves \(24 = 2^3 \times 3\). The 3 is a prime that is neither 2 nor 5, and it cannot cancel because the fraction is already in lowest terms. That single 3 is the troublemaker that keeps the decimal going. If you want proof, the long division of \(\tfrac{1}{24}\) settles into \(0.041\overline{6} = 0.041666\ldots\), a decimal that truly never ends. The denominator that repeats is \(\boxed{24}\).

review.x: Find \(0.\overline{81} - 0.\overline{36}\) as a single fraction in lowest terms. Both two-digit repeaters sit over \(99\); subtract the numerators and reduce.
hints: A two-digit repeating block sits over two nines. So \(0.\overline{81}=\tfrac{81}{99}\) and \(0.\overline{36}=\tfrac{36}{99}\). Now you have two fractions that share the same bottom.
When the denominators already match, you only subtract the tops. Work out \(81-36\), keep \(99\) underneath, then look for a common factor to reduce the result.
review.sol: Every repeating block of two digits turns into that block over two nines, so \(0.\overline{81}=\dfrac{81}{99}\) and \(0.\overline{36}=\dfrac{36}{99}\). They were built on the same denominator, which is the lucky part. Subtracting is just subtracting the numerators. $$\frac{81}{99}-\frac{36}{99}=\frac{81-36}{99}=\frac{45}{99}$$ Now reduce. Both \(45\) and \(99\) share the factor \(9\), since \(45=9\times 5\) and \(99=9\times 11\). Dividing top and bottom by \(9\) gives \(\dfrac{45}{99}=\dfrac{5}{11}\). You can sanity check it by dividing \(5\) by \(11\), which loops as \(0.454545\dots=0.\overline{45}\), exactly the gap between the two decimals you started with. \(\boxed{5/11}\)
